actions.thought: I am on the button with A7s, which is a strong hand in a short-handed (3-player) game. Opening the pot is the standard strategic play to seize initiative and pressure the blinds. I will raise to 250, which is 2.5x the big blind, a standard opening size that balances value and fold equity.

5h2h is an extremely weak starting hand. Facing a 2.5x raise from the Button, and being out of position with the Big Blind yet to act, the optimal and standard play is to fold and preserve chips for better spots.

I am in the Big Blind with Td 4h. The Button (Flashlight) has raised to 250, and the Small Blind has folded. I need to call 150 more to see a flop in a pot that will be 550. While the pot odds are roughly 27%, T4o is a very weak hand with poor playability and equity realization, especially when playing out of position against the original raiser. Defending with such a marginal hand is generally -EV in the long run. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Q7 offsuit is a weak hand even 3-handed from the button. It lacks both playability and raw equity to justify a raise or a call. Folding pre-flop is the standard and most profitable play to preserve chips for better spots.

I am in the Small Blind with 4h 2c, which is a very weak starting hand. The Button (Pro) has already folded. While I only need to put in 50 more to see a flop, 4-2 offsuit is rarely worth playing out of position against the Big Blind. Folding is the standard disciplined play to avoid losing more chips with a bottom-tier hand.

In a three-handed game, Ace-Four offsuit is a strong enough hand to open for a raise from the button. My goal is to either take down the blinds immediately or play a pot in position. A standard 2.5x raise to 250 is appropriate here to exert pressure while maintaining a manageable pot size.

A9 suited is a strong hand to defend from the small blind against a button open. Calling is the optimal play here to maintain a controlled pot size and see a flop, as 3-betting with A9s OOP against the button risks ballooning the pot with a marginal holding early in the tournament. I need to commit a total of 250 chips to match the current raise.

K2s is a very weak suited King. Facing a 2.5x raise from the Button and a call from the Small Blind, we are getting decent pot odds, but we will be playing multiway out of position with a hand that suffers from significant reverse implied odds if we pair our King. It's best to fold and preserve our stack for better spots.

The flop of Ks-Qc-4c is disconnected from my hand, though I hold an Ace-high with a backdoor flush draw. As the pre-flop caller out of position, checking is the standard play to let the aggressor (the Button) act. This allows me to control the pot size and re-evaluate based on their response to the board. Betting here would be an unnecessary bluff as my equity is relatively low.

I have bottom pair with an Ace kicker on a board that is quite heavy with broadway cards (King and Queen). Since Flashlight checked to me, checking back is a prudent play to control the pot size and realize my showdown value. The board texture hits a lot of a caller's range from the Small Blind, and by checking, I avoid a potential check-raise while keeping the pot manageable with a marginal hand.

With Ace-high and no strong draw, the board remains uncoordinated. Since my opponent checked behind on the flop, they likely don't have a strong King or Queen. Checking again keeps the pot manageable and allows me to see if they check again or make a small bet, at which point I can decide to call or fold. Betting at this point is unnecessary since I have little fold equity and no value.

I have bottom pair (Fours) with an Ace kicker on a board with two broadway cards (K, Q). Flashlight has checked twice, suggesting they might have a weak hand or are playing a medium hand cautiously. By checking behind on the turn, I maintain pot control and ensure I can see the river. My hand has showdown value, and betting risks a check-raise or being called only by better hands. I will take the free card and re-evaluate on the river.

The board texture is K-Q-4-6-4. I hold A-9 high, which has very little showdown value, but the opponent's check-behind on both the flop and turn suggests they also do not have a strong hand (like a King or a Queen). Betting now would be a pure bluff against a range that might contain some marginal pocket pairs or air that I am already tying or beating. Given that betting offers little fold equity and checking allows me to take the hand to showdown without risking more chips on a likely losing hand, checking is the most sound strategic play.

I have improved to three-of-a-kind (Fours) with an Ace kicker on the river. Flashlight has checked through every street, suggesting a weak or medium-strength hand like a Queen, a weak King, or a mid-pocket pair. Since I checked back on the flop and turn, my hand looks relatively weak, making a value bet highly effective here. I will bet 300, which is half the pot, to extract value from those medium-strength hands that may call thinking I am taking a stab at the pot late.

The opponent checked both the flop and turn, which typically indicates a range skewed towards draws that missed or weak made hands. However, by betting 300 into a 500 pot on the river, they are now representing at least some showdown value or attempting to polarize their range. With Ace-high (A9), my hand is strictly a bluff catcher. Given the board (K-Q-4-6-4) offers many opportunities for the opponent to have a small pair (like 55, 77, 88 or a 6) that they decided to turn into a value bet, my A9 is likely beaten. Calling here requires a high frequency of bluffs from the opponent, which I cannot guarantee. Therefore, folding is the most disciplined and conservative play to preserve my stack.

Queen-Five offsuit is a very weak starting hand, especially in a three-handed game. There is no incentive to play this hand from the button as it performs poorly post-flop and provides very little equity against opponents. Therefore, folding is the most disciplined and profitable play to conserve my stack.

With pocket sixes in the small blind after the button folds, I have a strong starting hand that plays well in heads-up situations. I should raise to build the pot, take the betting lead, and put pressure on the big blind. A raise to 300 (3x the big blind) is a standard and effective sizing.

I have Ace-Six offsuit in the Big Blind. The Small Blind raised to 300. In a 3-handed game, the Small Blind's opening range is typically very wide. A6o is a solid hand to defend with, especially since I will have position on the Small Blind for the rest of the hand. Calling allows me to see a flop and utilize my positional advantage without bloating the pot unnecessarily against a range that could contain stronger Aces or pairs.

The flop is 7h Th Qd, which hits the big blind caller's range very hard. Flash can have numerous straight draws (J9, KJ, 89), flush draws, and paired hands (Tx, Qx, 7x, or higher pocket pairs). Our pocket sixes are an underpair to the board with almost no potential to improve, and being out of position, betting as a bluff is unlikely to be profitable. I will check and evaluate, intending to fold to any bet.

On a flop of 7h-Th-Qd, the board is quite connected and hits a lot of a typical pre-flop raising range. Since Pro (SB) checked, they might be playing a marginal hand cautiously or missed. However, my Ace-high has very little equity to continue if I get raised, and betting doesn't accomplish much against hands that likely called/raised pre-flop. Checking back allows me to see a turn for free and keep the pot small, maintaining my positional advantage.

The turn brings a King, making the board 7h Th Qd Kc. This connects heavily with my opponent's range, completing straights for any Jack and hitting many broadway hands. My pocket sixes are now vastly outclassed as a low underpair to the board. Without a realistic chance to win at showdown or successfully bluff, my best option is to check, fully prepared to fold if facing a bet.

I am currently holding Ace-high (As 6d) on a board of 7h-Th-Qd-Kc. This board is very coordinated and favors many hands in a Small Blind's opening range. However, Pro has checked both the flop and the turn. This passivity suggests they may have a marginal hand or have completely missed. While Ace-high has little showdown value here, betting might not force out better hands like a pair of tens or sevens, and I have a straight draw if a Jack hits the river (A-K-Q-J-T). Checking back allows me to see the river for free, maintain control over the pot size, and potentially realize my equity or decide on a bluffing opportunity depending on the river card.

The river brings a six, giving me a beautifully disguised bottom set! Given the check-check action on both the flop and the turn, it is highly unlikely Flash holds a straight (any Jack or 98) or a higher set, as they would have almost certainly bet those strong hands for value or protection. My set of sixes is overwhelmingly likely to be the best hand. Checking might induce a bluff, but betting directly targets weak pairs like a Ten, a Seven, or a stubbornly played King or Ace-high that would simply check behind. A half-pot bet of 300 is an optimal sizing to extract value from these marginal hands.

Pro raised pre-flop but checked both the flop and the turn on a very coordinated board (7h-Th-Qd-Kc). This passivity suggests they likely do not have a strong hand like a King, Queen, or a straight, as those hands would typically bet for value or protection on such a connected board. The 6s on the river gave me bottom pair. Pro's 300-chip bet into a 600-chip pot provides me with 3-to-1 pot odds, meaning I only need to be right 25% of the time to break even. Given that Pro's line of checking twice and then betting the river often represents a polarized range of either a thin value bet (like a 7 or a 10) or a complete bluff with missed draws (like AJ, 98, or hearts), my pair of sixes is a viable bluff-catcher. I will call to see the showdown.

Holding a small pocket pair (33) on the button in a 3-handed game, I want to open with a standard raise to try to steal the blinds or take the lead in the hand. Raising to 2.5x the big blind is a standard opening size.

I have Ace-Ten offsuit in the Small Blind, which is a strong hand in a three-handed game. The Button (Pro) has opened for 2.5x the big blind. I want to take the initiative and potentially end the hand now or isolate the Button. A 3-bet is the standard play here to avoid playing a multi-way pot out of position against the Big Blind. I will raise to 800 to put pressure on the opener and the Big Blind.

I hold pocket kings, an premium hand. The button (Pro) opened, and the small blind (Flash) 3-bet to 800. In this situation, I must take control of the pot. A 4-bet is the optimal play to maximize value and define the strength of my hand. Limiting the field or building a large pot now gives me an advantage for the subsequent streets.

I opened 33 from the Button, but was met with a 3-bet from the Small Blind and a cold 4-bet from the Big Blind. The action is extremely strong, indicating premium holdings from at least one, if not both, opponents. Calling 2150 chips to set-mine is mathematically unprofitable given effective stacks, as I am not getting the required 10:1 implied odds. Folding is the only correct decision here.

I originally 3-bet to 800 with Ace-Ten offsuit to take control of the pot. However, Flashlight (Big Blind) has cold 4-bet to 2400. In a 3-handed game, a cold 4-bet from the Big Blind represents a very narrow and strong range, typically JJ+, AK, and occasionally AQ. Ace-Ten is dominated by most of this range. Calling 1600 more would leave me out of position with a dominated hand in a bloated pot. Folding is the disciplined play here to preserve my stack.

In a 3-handed game, T8o is a strong enough hand to open from the button. I want to exert pressure and take advantage of my positional edge post-flop. A standard 2.5x raise is appropriate here to test the blinds.

Nine-Jack offsuit is a relatively weak starting hand to defend from the small blind against a button open. Playing out of position with this hand leads to difficult post-flop scenarios. Folding is the most disciplined and strategic play to preserve my stack.

Facing a 2.5x open from the Button. While my hand is suited and connected (4s 3s), it is simply too low to profitably defend out of position, even against a wide Button opening range. Folding is the standard and safest play to preserve my stack for better spots.

I am in the button position with 8c 5s. This is a very weak hand with poor playability and low equity. Opening the pot with such a hand is generally unprofitable, and there is no reason to limp given the strength of the hand. Folding is the most disciplined and standard play here to preserve my stack.
J2o is a very weak, disconnected hand. Playing it out of position against the big blind is a losing play in the long run. I will fold and save my chips for better opportunities.

Q3s is too weak to open from the Button even in 3-handed play. The risk of being dominated by better Queens or failing to connect well post-flop makes folding the most profitable and lowest variance choice here. I will save my chips for a stronger starting hand.
Folded to me in the small blind with Jack-Ten offsuit. This is a strong enough hand to raise for value and fold equity against the big blind. I will raise to 3x the big blind to 300.

I am in the big blind with King-Jack suited, a strong hand for heads-up play against a small blind open. The small blind's opening range is likely wide, so 3-betting to 950 allows me to capture the initiative, build a pot with a strong holding, and leverage my position post-flop.

I opened to 300 from the small blind with Jack-Ten offsuit, and Flashlight 3-bet me to 950 from the big blind. While JTo is a reasonable opening hand, it plays poorly against a 3-bet range when out of position. Calling 650 more would commit a significant portion of my stack to a pot where I am likely dominated or facing a coin flip at best, and I will have to play the rest of the hand without position. Given the tournament context and the strength of a typical 3-bet from the big blind, folding is the most disciplined and prudent play.

I am on the button with Ten-Seven offsuit. While 3-handed play allows for a wider opening range, T7o is near the bottom of my opening range from the button. Given that both opponents have shown significant aggression in previous hands (e.g., Flashlight's 4-bet in Hand #5 and 3-bet in Hand #8), I expect to be played back at frequently. I will choose the conservative route and fold this weak hand to preserve my stack for more favorable spots.

Holding 3c-9d is a very weak starting hand, and playing it out of position against the Big Blind is negative expected value in the long run. Since the Button has folded, I am facing a blind-versus-blind situation. Folding is the most disciplined and stack-efficient move given the poor strength of the hand.

I am holding 6-10 offsuit on the button, which is a weak hand that lacks sufficient equity and playability to warrant an open-raise or a call. With 86 big blinds effective, playing mediocre hands out of position or in marginal spots is not optimal. I will fold to conserve my stack for a more favorable situation.
K9o is a solid hand in a blind-vs-blind situation. Raising to 300 puts pressure on the Big Blind, giving us fold equity pre-flop, and allows us to take the initiative if called.

I am holding Ten-Nine offsuit in the Big Blind. The Small Blind (Pro) has raised to 300. In a three-handed game, T9o is a solid hand to defend with, especially since I will have direct position over the Small Blind for the remainder of the hand. The pot odds are 2:1 (200 to call into a 400 pot), which is more than enough to justify seeing a flop.

The board of 7d 8h Jd heavily favors the Big Blind's calling range, which contains many straight draws, flush draws, and pair+draw combinations. While I have a gutshot straight draw (any Ten gives me a straight) and an overcard with a backdoor flush draw, c-betting out of position into this wet board is likely to get called or raised frequently. Checking for pot control and to evaluate the opponent's action is the best play.

I have flopped the nut straight (7-8-9-10-J). Although there is a flush draw on the board with two diamonds, I am in a very strong position. Pro, the pre-flop aggressor, has checked to me. I should bet here to build the pot and extract value from worse hands like top pair, two pair, or even flush draws that might call. A bet of 400 (two-thirds of the pot) is a standard value bet in this situation.

Our opponent bets 2/3 of the pot on a highly coordinated and wet board (7d 8h Jd) that connects extremely well with a Big Blind's pre-flop defending range. We hold King-high with a weak gutshot straight draw (a Ten gives us the low end of the straight, and any diamond could complete a flush) and an overcard that might not be clean. Calling out of position with such low equity and reverse implied odds is a losing play. The optimal decision is to fold.
I am on the button with 73o, which is a very weak unplayable hand. The best decision is to fold pre-flop.

I am in the Small Blind with 6h 4d, which is a very weak starting hand. The Button (Pro) has folded. Flashlight in the Big Blind has shown aggression in previous hands (e.g., Hand 5 and Hand 8). Attempting to steal or play a weak hand out of position against an active Big Blind is not optimal strategy. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

K8o is a standard opening hand from the button in a 3-handed game. I'll raise to 2.5bb (250 chips) to put pressure on the blinds and utilize my positional advantage. If I get called, I'll have the advantage of acting last on all subsequent streets.

I am in the Small Blind with 6s 2c, which is a very weak hand with no playability. Calling 200 chips to see a flop out of position against an aggressive button raiser is negative expected value. Folding is the correct decision to preserve my stack.

Flash opened from the Button to 2.5bb. We are in the Big Blind with 8s7s, a premium suited connector. We are getting good pot odds (150 to call to win 400) and this hand plays very well post-flop, offering great straight and flush potentials against a wide Button opening range. A call is standard to defend the Big Blind and see a flop.

With middle pair, a gutshot straight draw, and a backdoor flush draw, I have decent equity but my hand is not strong enough to donk bet into the pre-flop aggressor on such a wet, coordinated board. Checking allows me to control the pot size, evaluate the Button's continuation bet, and decide whether to call or potentially check-raise depending on their sizing.

I have middle pair (8s) with a King kicker on a highly coordinated board (5s 9h 8h). While betting could provide protection against draws, checking back is a safer pot-control measure. It allows me to realize my showdown value without bloating the pot against 9x hands or getting pushed off my equity by a check-raise on such a wet board. I will see how the turn develops.

The turn brings the Ace of Hearts, putting three hearts on the board and completing flush draws, while also hitting many overcards in the pre-flop raiser's range. I have third pair (eights) and a gutshot straight draw, but no heart in my hand to draw to a flush. Betting here makes little sense, as I would only be called by better hands (Aces, higher pairs, or flushes) and I have very little fold equity. Checking allows me to control the pot size and re-evaluate if Flash bets.

On the turn, the Ace of hearts has improved my hand by giving me a King-high flush draw to go with my pair of eights. The Big Blind (Pro) has checked twice. While I could bet for value or to deny equity, checking back is a lower-variance play that allows me to realize my showdown value or hit my flush draw for free. If I bet and get check-raised, I would be in a difficult spot despite my equity. By checking, I control the pot size and can see a river card without further investment.

The river is a brick (Deuce of Spades). We have third pair (eights) with a seven kicker. Both players checked the flop and the turn, indicating marginal strength or complete air. Our pair of eights has decent showdown value against missed straight draws, King-high, and Queen-high combinations in the Button's range. However, it is not strong enough to value bet, as we would only be called by better hands like a nine, an Ace, or a slow-played flush. Checking to get to a cheap showdown is the most logical play. If Flash bets, we will likely fold since we beat very few value bets and bluff-catching with third pair on a three-flush board is risky.

On the river, the board is 5s 9h 8h Ah 2s. I have a pair of eights with a King kicker. The Big Blind (Pro) has checked for the third time. My hand has decent showdown value against missed draws (like 67 or 7T) and small pocket pairs or a 5. However, if I bet, I am unlikely to get called by a worse hand, and I would only be called or raised by hands that beat me (like an Ace, a 9, or a flush). Given the action, checking back is the most prudent play to realize my showdown value in a small pot.

Jack-Eight offsuit is a marginal hand, but on the button in a three-handed game, it is often strong enough to attempt a steal of the blinds. I will open to 250 to apply pressure on the small and big blinds.

QJo is an offsuit broadway hand that performs poorly when played out of position from the Small Blind. Calling a Button raise with it often leads to dominated spots post-flop (e.g., against AQ, KQ, AJ). Since we are out of position and still have the Big Blind behind us, folding is the most solid and standard play to avoid marginal situations.

I am in the Big Blind with King-Queen of diamonds, which is a premium suited broadway hand. Flashlight opened from the Button to 2.5x BB. Pro folded in the Small Blind. KQs plays extremely well post-flop, especially in a heads-up pot. While I could 3-bet for value and to take the initiative, calling is also a strong option to keep Flashlight's wider range in and realize equity. However, in a 3-way tournament dynamic, establishing dominance with high-equity hands is beneficial. Given the stack sizes and position, a 3-bet to around 850-950 would be standard, but a call is perfectly acceptable to see a flop. I will choose to raise to 900 to build a pot with a superior hand and potentially take it down pre-flop.

I opened the button with Jack-Eight offsuit to steal the blinds, but Flash has 3-bet to 900. J8o is far too weak to defend against a 3-bet, as it is dominated by most of the opponent's continuing range. Calling would be a mistake as it forces me to play a bloated pot with a weak hand that has poor equity. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

K8s is a solid suited hand that plays well in position. Opening from the Button in a 3-handed game with this hand is a standard aggressive play to either steal the blinds or take control of the hand going into the flop.

In a 3-handed game, QTo is a solid hand to defend the Small Blind against a standard 2.5x open from the Button. While playing out of position is not ideal, the hand has good equity against a wide button opening range. I will call the 200 to see a flop, keeping the pot manageable.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Eight offsuit, facing a raise from the button and a call from the small blind. The pot odds are very favorable (calling 150 to win a pot that will be 750), and Ace-Eight offsuit is a standard defend in this spot given the position and the fact that I am closing the action pre-flop.

On a low, disconnected flop of 6h-3s-4c, my Qs Th has completely missed. I am out of position against two opponents, one of whom was the pre-flop aggressor (Pro) and the other who likely has many low-to-middle cards in their defending range (Flashlight). Betting here into two players is unlikely to succeed often enough to be profitable, and I have no real draw to justify building a pot. I will check to see how the action develops; if there is significant betting, I will likely fold.

The board is low and disconnected (6-3-4), which is unlikely to have connected with my Ace-Eight offsuit. Betting into two opponents on this texture is ill-advised as it will likely only be called by better hands. I will check to keep the pot size manageable and potentially realize my equity if I turn an overcard or a backdoor straight draw.

I missed the flop entirely with King-high and only a backdoor flush draw. The 6-3-4 board connects well with the pre-flop calling ranges of the blinds, making it likely that at least one of them has a pair, straight draw, or combo draw. C-betting into two opponents on this low, connected texture is a low-percentage bluff. Checking back allows me to control the pot size, realize my equity for free, and re-evaluate on the turn.

I currently hold Queen-high with no direct draws on a board of 6-3-4-9. The flop went check-check-check, indicating that the other players likely didn't connect strongly with the low cards. However, with two opponents still in the hand and having no showdown value, there is little reason to lead out with a bet. I will check and hope to see a free river or fold if there is significant action.

I am holding Ace-high on a board that has checked around on the flop. The turn 9s doesn't improve my hand significantly, but it does make the board slightly wetter. Betting here into two opponents is unlikely to succeed as a bluff because I have limited equity and I am effectively representing very little strength. Checking is the standard play to control the pot size and attempt to reach a cheap showdown, especially since I still have a chance of having the best hand against other high-card hands.

With King-high and no draws on a coordinated turn board, bluffing into two opponents is risky, as the 9s or the low connected cards likely hit someone's calling range or gave them a pair/draw. Checking back again allows me to see a free river, retain my chips, and try to spike a King or an Eight, or potentially win a showdown against broken draws.

I have Queen-high (Qs Th) on a board of 6h-3s-4c-9s-5d. The pot has been checked through on both the flop and the turn, suggesting that no one has a particularly strong hand. However, the river 5d completes several potential straights (any 7 or 2) and connects the low board significantly. With two opponents still in the hand, bluffing into a pot that has shown zero interest is risky, as someone likely has a small pair or even Ace-high that would beat me and might call a small 'stab' bet. Since I have no showdown value but the pot is relatively small, I will check and hope the hand is checked around, though I expect to lose to almost any pair or better high card.

The board shows a straight on the board (3-4-5-6) with a 9, meaning any player holding a 7 or a 2 has a straight. Holding only Ace-high, I do not have a strong hand, and any bet I make will likely be called only by players holding at least a 7 or a 2, or by players who also have high-card hands (splitting the pot). Checking is the optimal strategy to reach showdown without risking additional chips with a marginal hand.

The river completes a 4-card straight on the board (3-4-5-6), meaning any 7 or 2 makes a straight. Both opponents have checked all three streets. I only have King-high, which actually has decent showdown value if both opponents were on missed flush or straight draws. Bluffing into two opponents on a 4-straight board is risky because any weak pair or random 7/2 might just call, and it's highly possible someone backed into a pair of fives or nines. Checking back to realize showdown value is the safest and most optimal play here.

I am on the button with 8h 4c, which is a very weak starting hand even in a three-handed game. While playing the button allows for a wider range of opening, 8-4 offsuit is near the bottom of my range and doesn't justify an open against active opponents like Flashlight and Pro, who have shown willingness to 3-bet and defend their blinds. Folding is the most prudent play here to preserve my stack.

I am in the small blind with a suited connector, 7c 6c. With the button having folded, I am heads-up against the big blind. Raising here is a standard aggressive play to potentially take the pot pre-flop or control the hand in position against the big blind. A raise to 250 is a reasonable sizing to put pressure on the big blind while keeping the pot manageable if called.

Ad Qs is a premium hand, especially in a Blind vs. Blind scenario where the Small Blind's opening range is very wide. 3-betting to 800 extracts value from dominated hands like worse Aces or weaker Broadways, and takes the initiative while having positional advantage post-flop.

I opened with 7c 6c from the small blind, and the big blind (Pro) 3-bet. While I am out of position, 7c 6c has good equity and playability post-flop. With a call of 550 to reach a total pot of 1600, the pot odds justify continuing to see a flop, where I can reassess based on the texture of the board.

I am in the small blind with a pair of 7s and an open-ended straight draw on a board of 9h-8s-7d. This board texture heavily favors my range as the pre-flop aggressor and caller, while it likely misses the opponent's 3-bet range (high cards/overpairs) significantly. With a pair of 7s and outs to a straight (needing a 5 or 10), I have massive equity against hands like overpairs (AA, KK, QQ) or AK. I will lead out with a bet of 800 (50% of the pot) to exert pressure and build the pot for value or to potentially force a fold from better high-card hands immediately.

The 9h-8s-7d flop heavily favors the pre-flop caller's range, connecting with many of their middle pocket pairs, suited connectors, and one-gappers. Flashlight's donk bet of 800 signals strength on a board where my Ad Qs completely missed. With only two overcards and no significant draws on a highly coordinated board, continuing against a half-pot lead would be unprofitable. Folding is the best choice to preserve my stack.

I am dealt five of hearts and deuce of clubs on the button. This is an extremely weak starting hand with very little equity or playability. Even with the positional advantage, there is no strategic justification for entering the pot with such cards against two opponents. Folding is the optimal decision to preserve my stack and wait for a more favorable situation.
KJs is a very strong hand, especially in a blind vs blind situation. By raising to 300, I can take the initiative, build the pot when I likely have the best hand, and put pressure on the Big Blind.

I am in the Big Blind with 93o. The Small Blind (Pro) has raised to 300. While I am getting 3:1 odds to call and will have position post-flop, 93o is a very weak hand with poor playability. It is better to wait for a stronger hand rather than defending with such a low-equity holding.

I am on the button 3-handed with QJo, a decent offsuit broadway hand. It plays well as an open-raise to steal the blinds or take control post-flop. I'll raise to 250 to apply pressure while keeping the sizing standard.

I am in the Small Blind holding 79 offsuit, which is a weak hand with poor playability. The Button has raised to 250. Calling would put me out of position for the rest of the hand with a marginal holding, and the Big Blind is still left to act. Given the stack sizes and the hand strength, folding is the standard and most profitable decision to avoid losing chips in a disadvantaged spot.

Ace-Ten offsuit is a strong hand to defend the Big Blind against a button raise. Given the stack depths and the pot odds, calling is the most balanced and strategic decision, allowing me to see a flop with a solid hand without over-committing prematurely.

I am in the Big Blind with middle pair (Tens with an Ace kicker). The board is somewhat wet with two spades and a Queen, which is well within the button's opening range. As I am out of position, checking is the standard play to control the pot size and gauge the opponent's strength before committing further chips. I will evaluate the opponent's reaction to my check.

I have top pair with a strong Jack kicker on a wet, draw-heavy board (two spades, connected cards like T and Q). The big blind checked, giving me the perfect opportunity to continuation-bet for value and protection. A bet around half-pot to two-thirds pot will charge flush draws and straight draws, while extracting value from worse Qx and Tx hands.

I am on the button with Jack-Nine offsuit. This is a marginal but playable hand in a steal position. Given the blinds are 50/100, raising to 300 is a standard opening size to apply pressure on the blinds and potentially win the pot pre-flop or take initiative into the flop.

7c 9d is a weak, unsuited two-gapper. Playing this hand out of position from the small blind against a pre-flop raise from the button is a losing play in the long run. Folding is the most standard and mathematically sound decision.

Qs7h is a very weak offsuit hand, and facing a raise from the Button, it is not strong enough to defend out of position. The best action is to fold and save the chips.

Holding 64s in the small blind facing a button raise, the hand is too weak to call given the positional disadvantage and the big blind still left to act. With Ace high and no direct draws on an uncoordinated board, checking behind is the standard play. My opponent's check could indicate weakness, but my hand still has some showdown value. Checking keeps the pot small and allows me to see a free turn card.

My opponent has checked twice on a board that doesn't heavily coordinate. The board texture is 8-3-9-K-8. My pocket sixes are counterfeit, as the best five-card hand I can make is the pair of eights on the board (K-9-8-8-3). Holding 6s-Th is a weak hand with no real potential. In a three-handed game, even on the button, this hand is not worth committing chips to in an unraised pot. I will fold and wait for a more playable hand to preserve my stack.

With King-Queen offsuit in the Small Blind after the Button folds, I have a very strong hand for a Blind vs Blind situation. A standard raise to 3x the Big Blind will build the pot and put pressure on the Big Blind, giving me the betting lead.

With Ace-Ten offsuit on the Button in a 3-handed game, I have a strong enough hand to open the pot. A standard raise to 3 big blinds will put pressure on the blinds and often win the pot uncontested or give me the initiative post-flop.

I am in the Big Blind with 4s9d. The pot is offering good odds to call (200 to win 700), and with another player having already called, defending the Big Blind is the standard play here despite the weak hand. I will call to see the flop.

I am in the big blind with a pair of 9s and a 4 kicker on a board of 9s-7s-Tc. I have top pair with top kicker on a very wet and coordinated board (9s 7s Tc). Both opponents have checked to me. On a 9s-7s-Tc board, I have an open-ended straight draw with my King-Eight. This gives me 8 outs to a straight (any Jack or Six). I have a pair of nines on a board of 9s-7s-Tc. While this is a weak pair with a poor kicker, the pot offers excellent odds (500 to win a pot of 1900, roughly 3.8:1). On the turn, the 2nd of diamonds is a brick that doesn't change the board texture. I still hold an open-ended straight draw with my King-Eight on the Nine-Seven-Ten board. The turn card (2 of diamonds) is a blank that does not help my hand. The turn brings a complete blank (2d), leaving my top pair with top kicker ahead of most of my opponents' ranges, which are likely heavy with straight and flush draws after calling the flop. On the turn, the board is 9s-7s-Tc-2d. I hold Ks-8h for an open-ended straight draw (8 outs: any Jack or Six). The pot is 2400 before Pro's bet, and Pro has bet 1200. Additionally, Flashlight is still in the hand; if Flashlight calls, the pot odds improve to 20% (1200 / 6000), which is very close to my actual equity. I will call to see the river.

My hand, a pair of 9s with a 4 kicker, is extremely weak in this three-way pot. The board (9s-7s-Tc-2d) is highly coordinated with straight and flush draw possibilities. Pro has shown consistent aggression by betting into two players on both the flop and the turn, and Flash has called both times, strongly suggesting both opponents hold better hands—likely sets, two-pair, or strong draws. On the river, the 6h completes a 10-high straight for me (6-7-8-9-10) using the 8h in my hand. If Pro has been bluffing or has a strong but non-straight hand like a set or two-pair, he is likely to fire a third barrel or bet for value. If I lead out (donk bet), I risk folding out his bluffs. By checking, I allow Pro to continue his aggressive line, and I can then decide whether to just call or check-raise depending on his bet size and my assessment of whether he's likely to call a raise with a worse hand on this somewhat coordinated board.

The river brings the 6h, putting a four-card straight (6-7-9-T) on the board. Any opponent holding an 8 now has a straight. Any missed spade flush draws will fold to a bet anyway, and it's unlikely we can get three streets of value from a worse ten like QT or JT given the scary board. Checking behind ensures we reach showdown without unnecessarily risking more chips when we are often behind.

Flashlight checked to me. I have King-high with no draws on a 6-4-8 rainbow board. While a bluff could occasionally fold out better high-card hands, checking back to realize my equity and see a free turn is a safe and standard play, keeping the pot small with a marginal holding.

Flashlight has checked twice, showing weakness, but the 6-4-8-5 board is highly connected and easily hits a Small Blind limping range. Eight-three suited is a very weak hand with poor board coverage and low equity against the ranges of the players in the blinds. 86o is a weak offsuit one-gapper. Playing this from the small blind out of position against the big blind is generally unprofitable. The best play is to fold and save my chips for better spots.

I am in the small blind with a very weak hand (6h 3d). There is no incentive to call a raise from the button with such a dominated and uncoordinated hand out of position. Folding is the most disciplined and mathematically correct choice.
5c 7h is a weak offsuit hand with very little playability. Facing a raise from the button, folding is the best decision to preserve my stack, especially since we are out of position.

I am dealt Jack-Six offsuit on the button. This is a weak, non-playable hand that does not warrant an investment of chips, even with the button advantage. Given the lack of strength in my hole cards, the most disciplined and profitable long-term decision is to fold and wait for a better spot.

With 4s3s in the small blind facing the big blind, the hand is a bit too weak to play consistently out of position. I have flopped top two pair with Queen-Ten on a relatively dry board of Queen-Ten-Five. The small blind has continuation bet, and this is a prime opportunity to build the pot for value. Folding would be too tight given the equity, and re-raising is unnecessary as I want to realize my draw's value.

I am out of position on the turn with an open-ended straight draw (8 outs). The 3d is a brick that shouldn't change the board texture significantly. I have top two pair with Queen-Ten on a board of Queen-Ten-Five-Three. This is an extremely strong hand. Flashlight's aggressive line (raising the flop and betting the turn) suggests a very strong hand, likely a set or two-pair. I am on the button with 5d 3s, which is a very weak hand. Even in a 3-handed game, this hand is well below the threshold for an opening raise. Both opponents are capable players, and there is no reason to put chips into the pot with such poor equity and playability.

I am in the Small Blind position with a very weak hand, 7d 4c. Since the Button has folded, I am heads-up against the Big Blind. Given the low card value and lack of coordination, there is no strategic advantage to playing this hand. I will fold to preserve my stack.

I am on the button with a weak hand (Jack-Four offsuit). This is a clear fold situation, as there is no reason to invest chips with such a marginal holding, especially when I can easily wait for a better spot to enter the pot.

Q3 offsuit is a weak, disconnected hand with poor playability. Playing it out of position against the big blind, who has a commanding stack size, is unprofitable. The best course of action is to simply fold and wait for a stronger starting hand.

We are on the Button (first to act in a 3-handed game) with A6o. This is a very standard opening hand to steal the blinds or play post-flop in position with Ace high. I will make a standard raise to 2.5x the big blind.

Holding Ace-Ten offsuit in the Big Blind against a button raise is a standard defense. The hand has strong high-card value and plays well against a wide opening range from the button. Calling the additional 150 chips allows me to see a flop with a favorable hand and proceed based on the texture, while keeping the pot manageable against an aggressive opponent.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Ten offsuit on a board of King-Queen-Four. This board is very strong for the pre-flop aggressor (the Button), who likely has many King-x or Queen-x combinations. The board of Ks Qc 4h is very favorable to my pre-flop opening range. If called, I still have an overcard, but I'll likely proceed with caution on later streets. A c-bet of 200 represents strength and often wins the pot right here.

The flop is King-Queen-Four, and I have Ace-Ten, giving me a gutshot straight draw (needing a Jack). The opponent's bet is small (200 into 750), which offers decent pot odds to peel one card. While I am likely behind their range, the potential to hit the straight or possibly pair the Ace makes calling the correct strategic play to see a turn card.

I have successfully completed a Broadway straight (Ace-King-Queen-Jack-Ten) on the turn. This is currently the nuts. On the turn, the board is Ks Qc 4h Jc, which is extremely coordinated and connects heavily with the Big Blind's defending range. I am on the button with K3o. While a 3-handed game allows for a wider opening range, K3o is a very weak hand that doesn't flop well and is easily dominated. Given my chip lead, I see no reason to play a marginal hand out of position or risk a 3-bet from the blinds. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

With the button having folded, I am heads-up against the big blind. As the small blind, I have 50 chips already in the pot, and the big blind has 100. Given the speculative nature of my suited cards (3h-Th), completing the small blind by calling the additional 50 is a standard play to see a flop and evaluate the situation cheaply.
With 34o in the big blind facing a small blind complete, I have a very weak hand and no incentive to bloat the pot. Checking allows me to see a free flop.

The board is paired with 7s-2s-7h, which is a very dry texture. I hold Ten-high with no pair and no significant draw. Betting here would be a pure bluff into the Big Blind's range, which likely contains some 7s, pocket pairs, or better high-card hands. Since I am out of position, checking is the standard and safest play to keep the pot small and re-evaluate on the turn if the situation improves.

I have 3-high on a paired 7-7-2 board. My opponent checked to me. I have virtually no equity and no compelling reason to bluff at this pot against a small blind complete. Checking behind to see a free turn is the standard and safest play.

The board is [7s, 2s, 7h, Qc]. I hold [3h, Th], which is ten-high and has zero showdown value. Since I am acting first on the turn after checking the flop, there is no benefit to betting into the Big Blind's range, as I have no equity. Checking allows me to potentially see the river for free or fold if my opponent shows aggression, which is the most prudent path given my lack of a hand.

With 4-high and absolutely no draw or showdown value on a 7-7-2-Q board, there is no reason to start bluffing against a small blind who might have slow-played a 7, hit a Queen, or holds a pair. Checking behind is the safest play to get to the river for free.

I am in the Small Blind with Ten-high on a board of 7s-2s-7h-Qc-6s. My hand has no showdown value, and there is no realistic chance of forcing a fold from the Big Blind who likely has at least a Queen or a pair of 7s or 6s in their range. Betting would be a pure bluff into a board that has missed me completely, and checking allows me to reach showdown or fold to a bet without risking more chips in a pot that offers very little reward.

I have 4-high and completely missed the board. The opponent has checked all three streets, indicating weakness, but they could easily still hold Ace-high, King-high, or a weak pair which would beat me at showdown. The pot is very small (2 BBs), so there is no significant reason to risk chips on a bluff. Checking and conceding the hand is the most standard, lowest-variance play.

I am in the button position with 6h-8c. This is a very weak, offsuit hand with poor connectivity and low card values. Given the 3-handed table, playing garbage hands from the button is generally suboptimal unless there is a specific read or reason to exploit the blinds. I will fold to conserve my stack and wait for a more favorable opportunity.

With 5h 4h in the Small Blind and the Button folding, I have a solid suited connector. Raising to 300 puts pressure on the Big Blind, giving me the betting initiative and a good chance to steal the blinds outright, or set up a continuation bet on the flop if called.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten-Nine offsuit. The Small Blind (Pro) has raised to 300, which is a standard 3x open. Given the pot odds (calling 200 to win a total pot of 600) and the fact that I will have position on Pro for the rest of the hand, this is a clear defend. T9o has decent connectivity and high-card value in a blind-vs-blind battle. I will call to see a flop.

I flopped trip fives on a very dry board (5-5-K). With such a strong hand, my goal is to extract maximum value. A small continuation bet of 1/3 pot (200) can keep my opponent's range wide, enticing calls from Ace-highs, small pocket pairs, or drawing him into floating. It also easily gets called by any King in his range. Checking might result in him checking behind, losing a street of value.

The flop of 5c 5d Kh is quite dry and favors the pre-flop raiser's range, which includes many Kings. I have Ten-high with no draws and very little equity to improve to a winning hand. While Pro's lead is small (1/4 pot), calling with nothing but 'backdoor' possibilities is generally losing in the long run, especially since I have no showdown value. I will fold and wait for a more favorable situation.

Ten-Seven suited is a marginal hand. Even though we are 3-handed and I am on the button, my stack is somewhat short relative to my opponents. I prefer to fold and preserve my chips for stronger holdings rather than open a borderline hand.

I am in the Small Blind with Jack-Six offsuit. The Button has folded, leaving only me and the Big Blind (Flashlight). While the pot odds to call (50 to win 150) are 3:1, Jack-Six offsuit is a very weak hand to play out of position. Given that I am currently the chip leader and we are only on Hand 53 of 150, I will choose to fold this marginal hand rather than risk chips in a blind-vs-blind battle with poor card strength.

I am on the button with Queen-Four offsuit. In a three-handed game, while the button should open a wide range, Q4o is generally too weak to open for a raise, especially with two active opponents in the blinds who have shown willingness to defend or three-bet. I will fold and wait for a better starting hand.

I am in the small blind and the button has folded, leaving me heads-up against the big blind. Given the position, this is a prime opportunity to raise and attempt to steal the blinds. Jack-six suited is a playable hand in this context, allowing me to either take down the pot immediately or see a flop in position.

I have Queen-Jack suited in the big blind facing a raise from the small blind. This is a very strong hand to defend with, especially since I will have position post-flop. A call is standard here as it keeps the pot size reasonable and allows me to realize my equity well. While 3-betting is an option, QJs plays excellently as a flat call in position.

I am in the small blind with bottom pair (pair of sixes) on a wet board (6h, 5h, 8s). The board texture offers many straight and flush draws that connect well with the big blind's calling range. Betting into this board is risky as I likely only get called by hands that have me beat or have significant equity against me. Checking is the most strategic play to control the size of the pot and evaluate the opponent's reaction, allowing me to fold if the pressure becomes too high or potentially check-call on later streets if the board remains safe.

The board is highly coordinated (6h 5h 8s) and connects heavily with many hands in my opponent's range. With JsQs, I only have two overcards and a backdoor flush draw. Betting as a bluff here is risky because my opponent will continue with a wide range of pairs, straight draws, and flush draws. Taking a free card by checking behind is the most prudent play, allowing me to potentially improve on the turn without risking more chips.

The board has become more dangerous with the Ace on the turn. My pair of sixes is likely behind if my opponent has any piece of this board, especially an Ace, which is well within their calling range. Betting here serves little purpose as better hands will call or raise, and worse hands will fold, offering no value. Checking is the safest play to keep the pot size controlled and see if I can get to a cheap showdown or fold if facing significant aggression on the river.

My opponent has checked twice, showing significant weakness. The turn Ace of Spades improves my hand drastically by giving me the second nut flush draw, along with my two overcards. Betting here as a semi-bluff is a highly profitable play, as it applies pressure on marginal hands that might fold, while I still retain excellent equity to win the pot on the river if I am called. A half-pot bet of 300 is sized perfectly to accomplish this.

The opponent has bet 300 into a pot of 600, giving me 3:1 pot odds. While the Ace on the turn is a dangerous card that likely hits the opponent's range, the pair of sixes still has some equity against air or a bluff. Since the pot odds are attractive and this is a relatively small bet in the context of our stacks, calling is the most rational approach to reach the river cheaply and evaluate the situation there, rather than folding immediately.

The opponent has bet 300 into the pot on the river. Given the previous analysis and the pot odds offered (calling 300 to potentially win a total pot of 1500), calling is the most rational approach. While my pair of sixes is weak, it has sufficient equity against potential bluffs or missed draws on this board texture, especially given the aggressive betting pattern earlier.

The river completes my flush, giving me the second nut flush with the Queen and Jack of Spades on an Ace-high spade board. Flashlight has checked to me again, which strongly caps their range, meaning they likely possess a straight, a weaker flush, or a top pair like an Ace. Checking behind would be a missed opportunity for value. A bet of around two-thirds of the pot will extract maximum value from worse hands while only losing to exactly the King of Spades.

I hold a pair of sixes on a board of 6h-5h-8s-As-4s. The board is very dangerous, as it offers multiple straight and flush possibilities, and an Ace is on the board. The opponent's betting pattern (checking the flop, then betting on both the turn and river) strongly indicates they have at least an Ace or a made straight/flush. My pair of sixes is essentially a weak bluff-catcher that is likely behind a significant portion of their range, and calling 800 into a 1200-chip pot with such a weak hand on this board texture is not mathematically sound. Therefore, folding is the most disciplined and correct strategic choice.

I am in the button position with 3c-4h, which is an extremely weak starting hand. There is no strategic value in playing this hand, as it has low equity and potential for dominated pairs or low straight draws that are difficult to play. Folding is the correct decision to preserve my stack size.

With 75o in the Small Blind, the hand is too weak to play, even in a blind vs blind situation. Being out of position against the Big Blind with an uncoordinated, weak offsuit hand makes this an easy fold.
I have JTs on the Button, a strong suited connector that plays very well post-flop. Raising to 2.5x (250) is a standard open to steal the blinds or build a pot in position.

I have been dealt the best possible starting hand, Pocket Aces. Pro has opened with a standard raise from the button. To maximize value and protect my hand, I will put in a 3-bet. A raise to 850 is a standard size for a 3-bet out of the blinds against a button open, and it builds the pot effectively while making it more difficult for the opponents to realize their equity with speculative hands.

I am holding 3cQs, which is an extremely weak, disconnected, offsuit hand. Facing a 3-bet from the small blind after an open from the button, my range should be very tight. Calling or playing this hand out of position is highly negative expectation, so I will fold to preserve my stack.

Facing a 3-bet from the small blind, JTs is a strong suited connector that we can profitably defend with in position. It has excellent post-flop playability and can flop strong combo draws, straights, or flushes. With decent pot odds and plenty of stack depth behind to maneuver, calling to play a pot in position is the optimal choice.

I have Pocket Aces on a relatively dry flop of 6s-9h-2s, with a spade flush draw present. Pro called my 3-bet pre-flop, suggesting a range that includes pocket pairs and strong high cards. I will lead with a continuation bet of 900 (half pot) to extract value from medium pairs like 77, 88, or TT, and to charge any spade flush draws or backdoors.

We flopped a strong Jack-high flush draw with two overcards to the board. Facing a half-pot continuation bet, we have the direct equity and implied odds to call in position. Raising could fold out worse hands or force us into an all-in situation against strong overpairs where we'd be an underdog, so flat calling to realize our equity and play the turn in position is the optimal play.

The turn 9c pairs the board, which is a card that could have improved some of Pro's calling range from the flop, such as 89s or 9Ts. However, my Pocket Aces are still significantly ahead of most of Pro's range, which includes hands like TT, JJ, QQ, and various spade flush draws. I will fire a second barrel of 1,800 (half pot) to continue extracting value from those pairs and to charge the draws. This sizing allows me to potentially commit the rest of Pro's stack on the river while maintaining a strong lead in the hand.

The turn pairs the board with a nine, but given Flash's pre-flop 3-betting range from the small blind, he is highly unlikely to have a nine (with the exception of exactly 99 or occasionally A9s). His range is heavily weighted toward big overpairs (AA, KK, QQ) and strong broadways. We hold a Jack-high flush draw and are facing a half-pot bet of 1,800. We need 25% equity to make a direct mathematically profitable call. While our 9 flush outs give us about a 19.5% chance to hit on the river, the implied odds of getting the rest of our 3,400 stack in and being called by an overpair make up for the slight direct odds deficit. Shoving over his bet doesn't generate enough fold equity against his strong perceived range, so flat calling to realize our flush equity in position is the optimal choice.

The river 8d is a relatively safe card, though it does potentially improve a hand like 88 to a full house. However, given the action, my Pocket Aces are still the most likely best hand. The pot is currently 7,200, and Pro has only 3,400 remaining in his stack. This creates a situation where Pro is significantly pot-committed with any decent pair in his range, such as TT, JJ, or QQ. By betting his remaining 3,400, I am putting him all-in and maximizing my value against those overpairs. While he could occasionally have a 9, the frequency of overpairs and missed draws in his calling range justifies a shove to ensure I capture his entire stack.

We missed both our flush draw and straight draw on the river. Holding only Jack-high, we have absolutely no showdown value against Flash's triple-barrel, all-in range which heavily represents overpairs or strong made hands. Facing an all-in bet for our remaining 3,400 chips, the only viable option is to fold and preserve our remaining stack for future hands.

I am on the button with 65o. This is a very weak hand. While opening from the button is standard with a wide range, 65o is generally outside the opening range, especially when the Big Blind (Pro) has a relatively short stack of 33BB and might be looking for a spot to shove or defend aggressively after losing a significant pot in the previous hand. There is no reason to risk chips with a bottom-tier hand in this situation.

I am in the Small Blind with a very weak hand (3s, 8c). Given that this is a heads-up situation against the Big Blind, the range should be wider than in other spots, but 3-8 offsuit is essentially at the bottom of the range and has very poor playability. Folding is the disciplined choice to conserve my stack for better opportunities.

My hand, 3s and Tc, is weak and unsuited, offering little potential for a strong post-flop outcome. As the dealer in a three-handed game, I should maintain a disciplined approach and not enter pots with marginal cards, especially when I can preserve my stack by folding pre-flop.

With QTo in the Small Blind and the Button folding, I have a strong enough hand to attack the Big Blind. Raising to 300 has been effective at generating folds from Flash in previous similar situations, allowing me to pick up the blinds without resistance. If called, QTo plays reasonably well post-flop, and I can take the initiative.

I am in the Big Blind with Qc 5c. The Small Blind (Pro) has raised to 300. With a stack of 16,000 and Pro being quite short at 3,150, I am getting 2:1 pot odds to call (200 more to win a total pot of 600). Suited Queens have decent equity against a wide Small Blind opening range, and I will have position on the Pro for the remainder of the hand. Calling is the most efficient play here to see a flop and utilize my position and chip lead.

With Qd Th on a 4c 9s Js flop, I have an open-ended straight draw giving me 8 outs to the nuts. As the pre-flop aggressor, a continuation bet is standard to maintain initiative and act as a semi-bluff. Betting half the pot (300) applies pressure, potentially folding out overcards and weak pairs, while building the pot in case I hit my straight on the turn.

On the flop of 4c 9s Js, I am facing a standard continuation bet of 300 from Pro into a 600 pot. I hold Qc 5c, giving me a backdoor flush draw and one overcard (Queen). I am getting 3:1 pot odds (300 to call for a total pot of 900), which requires roughly 25% equity. Given I have position on the short-stacked Pro, I will call this bet to see a turn card, as I may be ahead if they are C-betting with air, and I have potential to improve.

The 9d on the turn pairs the board, which is generally a poor card to continue bluffing. It improves any 9x in my opponent's calling range to trips and does not weaken their top pair (Jx) holdings. With an open-ended straight draw (QTo), my best play is to check, aiming for a free river card to hit my straight while preserving my remaining stack. If Flash bets, I can evaluate the sizing to decide if I have the pot odds to call.

The turn card pairs the 9, making the board 4c 9s Js 9d. My opponent (Pro), who was the pre-flop aggressor and c-bet the flop, has now checked. While I only have Queen-high, betting here into a relatively short stack who might be pot-controlling a marginal hand like a Jack, a mid-pair, or even Ace-high is risky. By checking back, I take a free card to see if I can improve to a Queen or a Five on the river, or potentially win a small pot at showdown if the Pro is also holding air.

The board has double paired with 4s and 9s. My Queen-high (playing 9-9-4-4-Q) will lose to any Ace-high, King-high, or pocket pair that Flash might have held on to. Since Flash called the flop and checked the turn, his range includes some weak pairs, busted flush draws, or busted straight draws. While a bet might fold out some Ace-highs or small pairs, I don't want to risk a significant portion of my remaining stack (2850) on a bluff that could easily be called by a stubbornly played Jack or a disguised 9. Checking preserves my stack for a better spot, and I can still win at showdown against worse busted draws if it checks through.

On a double-paired board of 9-9-4-4-J, my hand is effectively two pair (9s and 4s) with a Queen kicker. The Pro (SB) raised pre-flop and continuation-bet the flop, but has checked both the turn and the river. This sequence typically indicates a marginal hand like Ace-high, King-high, or a missed draw. While my Queen-high beats some of his 'air' hands, I lose to any Ace or King. However, betting into a short-stacked opponent who may feel compelled to call with Ace-high on this board is risky. Since I have some showdown value in a blind-vs-blind battle, checking back is the most prudent play to see the showdown without risking further chips.

I am on the Button with A4o. At 34.5 big blinds deep, this is a standard spot to attempt a blind steal. Open-raising to 2.5x (250) applies pressure to the blinds and allows me to either win the pot uncontested pre-flop or play in position with an Ace-high hand if called.

With Jack-Seven offsuit in the small blind, I am facing a 2.5x open from the button. Pro has a relatively short stack (32BB), making them more likely to commit or shove post-flop. J7o is a weak hand that plays poorly out of position and is well behind a standard button opening range. Folding is the most prudent play to preserve my chip lead.

I am in the big blind with Ace-Eight suited, a solid hand to defend against a button raise. The pot odds are favorable for a call, and with a larger stack than the opponent, calling to see a flop is a standard, low-risk strategic decision.

I have middle pair (Eights) on a relatively dry, rainbow board with a Queen. As the pre-flop caller out of position, checking to the original aggressor (the button) is the standard approach to assess their hand strength and pot control. Leading out would not be optimal as I do not want to inflate a pot against a range that includes overcards and stronger pocket pairs.

On a board of Qc-8d-3c, my Ace-high has very little showdown value but there are plenty of worse hands in the Big Blind's range (like King-high, Jack-high, or weak straight draws without a club) that will fold to a continuation bet. A small c-bet of roughly 1/3 pot is cheap and effective here to fold out those hands and take the pot right away. If called, I will likely give up unless I improve.

I have middle pair with an Ace kicker on a relatively dry, rainbow board. Pro's bet of 200 into a 550 pot is a small continuation bet, which is a common play. My pair of eights is currently likely ahead of Pro's bluffs or weaker pocket pairs, and folding would be premature given the pot odds. Raising would likely only get called by hands that beat me, such as a Queen, so calling is the most balanced and value-preserving play to see the turn and evaluate further.

I have two pair (Aces and Eights) on an Ace-high board. The turn checked through, which indicates that neither I nor the opponent (Pro) has a very strong hand like a set or a high-ranking Ace, as they likely would have bet for value. Since I am acting first on the river, I should bet for value to extract chips from weaker hands like a single Queen or a weaker Ace. A bet of 500, roughly half the pot, is a solid value-bet size that encourages calls from those weaker hands while limiting my risk if the opponent happens to be trapping.

Flashlight check-called the flop and leads the turn when the Ace falls. I hit top pair, though with a weak kicker (4d). A lead here is often a semi-bluff with a draw (diamonds or clubs) or a weaker Ace. Since I have top pair, I can't fold to a single bet, but my kicker is too weak to raise for value against a donk bet. A call allows me to keep his bluffs and weaker Aces in the hand while controlling the pot size.

The river paired the Queen, which significantly strengthens the opponent's range if they hold a Queen, as they would have trips. Additionally, any Ace with a kicker higher than my Eight would beat me. Betting now would likely only induce calls from hands that have me defeated and cause the opponent to fold their bluffs. Checking is the most professional way to control the pot, avoid value-betting into a stronger hand, and potentially induce a bluff, allowing me to reach showdown efficiently.

The river pairs the board with a Queen, leaving me with two pair (Aces and Queens) and an 8 kicker, as my 4 is counterfeited by the 8 on the board. If Flashlight has a weak Ace (A2-A7), we will simply chop the pot. If he has a better Ace (A9+), he beats me. Any worse hand, such as a pair of 8s or missed club draws, will simply fold to a bet. Therefore, there is absolutely zero value in betting since I would only be called by hands that beat me or chop with me. Checking behind allows me to safely get to showdown and realize my equity.

I am on the button with Ah 9h, which is a very strong hand in a three-handed game. My stack is significantly larger than both opponents, especially Pro who is sitting at around 33 big blinds. A standard open-raise is the best play here to take control of the pot and apply pressure on the blinds. I will raise to 250.

I am in the small blind with a weak, speculative hand (8h, 5h). Calling a button raise out of position with this hand is generally negative expected value. It is better to fold and wait for a more advantageous spot, especially since I am out of position against an aggressive opponent.

I am in the Big Blind with Qs 6d, an offsuit, disconnected, and weak hand. Flash has raised from the Button, and I am out of position. Given my short stack relative to the other players, it is crucial to preserve my chips for hands with better playability and equity. Calling or raising here with Q6o is a losing play in the long run.

I am holding Queen-Six offsuit on the button. This is a weak starting hand with poor playability, especially given the tournament setting where maintaining a healthy stack is crucial. I prefer to wait for a stronger hand to enter the pot rather than opening with a marginal holding.

Holding T5o in the small blind is a very weak hand. Since Flashlight folded, completing or raising with trash out of position against the big blind is an unnecessary risk. Folding is the best choice to preserve our stack.

I am on the Button with Ace-Seven suited, which is a strong starting hand in this position. The pot is unopened. My stack is 33 BBs, so I want to be aggressive but control the pot size. A standard opening raise of 2.5x the big blind (250) is optimal here to steal the blinds or play in position post-flop.

I have Ace-Jack offsuit in the Small Blind. This is a very strong hand in a three-handed game. The Button (Pro) has raised to 250. With a stack of over 16,000, I am the chip leader. Pro has a relatively short stack of about 30 Big Blinds. I will put in a 3-bet to 850 to put pressure on Pro and isolate the pot, as calling would give the Big Blind (Flashlight) a very good price to enter and would leave me playing out of position against two players.

I am in the big blind facing a raise from the button and a 3-bet from the small blind. My hand, Ace-Nine suited, is decent but marginal when playing out of position against a 3-bet. The small blind (Flash) has been very aggressive and holds a large stack, making this a risky spot to continue. Folding is the disciplined play to preserve my stack and avoid a difficult, potentially negative expected value situation out of position.

Flash has consistently 3-bet to 850 from the small blind against my button opens. With A7s and around 33 BBs remaining, calling here commits a significant portion of my stack and creates a low SPR post-flop situation where I'll often miss and face heavy pressure. A 4-bet shove is too loose given the risk to my tournament life. Folding is the disciplined and optimal choice.

I am on the button with Jack-Deuce offsuit (J2o). This is a very weak hand, sitting at the bottom of the range. While I am the chip leader and could attempt to steal the blinds, the Big Blind (Pro) is relatively short-stacked with less than 30 big blinds and might look to shove or defend aggressively. Given my massive lead and the poor quality of the hand, there is no reason to get involved in this pot. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

I am in the Small Blind position after the button folds. Queen-Nine offsuit is a playable hand in a blind-versus-blind scenario. Since the Big Blind is short-stacked, raising might induce an unnecessary shove that would force me to fold. Calling the additional 50 chips is a low-risk way to see a flop and realize my equity.

With a low pocket pair like 44 in the big blind facing a small blind limp, checking is a standard and low-risk play. It guarantees a free look at the flop to try and hit a set, while preserving our relatively short stack (around 30 BBs). We will have position post-flop and can comfortably fold if we miss the board.

I am in the Small Blind position with a gutshot straight draw (needing a seven for a 6-7-8-9-10 straight) and a Queen high. The board of 8-10-6 is coordinated, and betting into the Big Blind out of position with just a draw is not optimal. Checking allows me to re-evaluate based on the opponent's action and control the pot size while seeing a turn card relatively cheaply.

With an underpair to the board (8h Ts 6d) and facing a check from the small blind, my best option is to check behind. Betting here would likely only get called by better hands (like an 8, 6, or a straight draw) and fold out hands we are already beating (like random unpaired cards). Taking a free card allows us to realize our equity or pot control to a showdown.

The turn card (Ten of Hearts) pairs the board. My hand is Queen-high with a gutshot straight draw (needing a 7). Since both the flop and turn have been checked through, it is unlikely either of us has a strong hand. Betting now might be interpreted as a probe bet, but given the board is paired, checking is the safer option to control the pot size. I will check to see a free river card, maintaining my ability to fold if my opponent decides to bet.

With two pair on the board (Tens and Fours) and facing a second check from the small blind, there is still very little value in betting. Betting would only get called by better pairs (like an 8, a 6, or a higher pocket pair) or draws, while folding out hands we already beat. Checking again keeps the pot small, controls the risk, and allows us to see the river for free to evaluate our showdown value.

I am holding Queen-high with no pair on a board of 8-T-6-T-A. The hand has been checked through on all streets, suggesting neither player has a particularly strong holding. With no showdown value, I have no reason to value bet. Betting as a bluff is unlikely to force a fold from any pair, and my opponent might be holding a small pair or a ten, so checking is the most prudent decision to minimize losses in a checked-down pot.

On the river, an Ace hits the board. Flashlight checks for a third time. My pocket fours give me two pair (Tens and Fours with an Ace kicker), but betting here has no value as we would likely only get called by better hands, such as a 6, an 8, or an Ace. We still have showdown value against missed straight draws or random high-card combinations. Checking behind to realize our showdown value and secure the small pot is the safest and most optimal play.

Queen-Three offsuit is a very weak hand with low playability. Even in the button position, there is no justification for investing chips into the pot with this holding. Folding is the disciplined play to preserve my stack.

With 3h 5h in the Small Blind against an aggressive Big Blind who covers me significantly, and with a relatively short stack of 31 big blinds, playing a suited one-gapper out of position is a losing proposition. Folding is the safest and most optimal choice to preserve my stack.

84s is a weak suited 3-gapper. Being the short stack with about 31 big blinds, we need to preserve our chips for better spots rather than opening such speculative hands, even from the button. The correct play is to simply fold and wait for a stronger starting hand.

I have Ace-Ten offsuit in the Small Blind. After the Button (Pro) folded, it's a blind-versus-blind situation. ATo is a very strong hand in this scenario. I will raise to 300 to exert pressure on Flashlight in the Big Blind and build a pot with what is likely the best hand.

I am holding a very weak hand, 2h 4s, which has minimal equity against almost any raising range. While I am getting 1:3 pot odds to call, it is not worth investing chips into such a poor hand out of position. Folding is the most disciplined and effective long-term strategy here.

I am on the button with Ace-Jack offsuit (AJo), which is a very strong starting hand in 3-handed play. I have a significant chip lead over both opponents, especially Pro who is sitting on a short stack of about 30 big blinds. I will open with a standard raise to 300 to exert pressure and build a pot with a premium hand.

Holding Ace-Six offsuit in the small blind against a button raise from an aggressive player is a weak position. Calling would require playing out of position against a range that likely dominates me, and with the big blind still to act, there is a risk of getting squeezed. Folding is the disciplined choice to preserve my stack.

Queen-Four offsuit is a very weak hand, well outside of any optimal defense range from the Big Blind against a 3x Button open. With a relatively short stack of 30 BBs, playing trash hands out of position is a quick way to bleed chips. Folding is the only sensible option here.

Ace-Five offsuit is a solid hand to open from the button in a 3-handed game. By raising, I can either take down the blinds or play in position. A raise to 300 is a standard opening size based on the previous hand history.
