A standard raise to 300 is appropriate here to induce a call or a re-raise from Flashlight, who has a significant stack.

I am in the big blind with 9s 4d against a small blind open raise. This is a very weak hand with poor playability, and although the pot odds are 2:1, it is generally better to fold such a trash hand rather than play it out of position against an aggressive opponent. I will preserve my stack for a better spot.

I am on the button with King-high. In a three-handed game, this is typically strong enough to open for a raise to steal the blinds or play a pot in position. The Big Blind (Pro) is short-stacked with about 20 big blinds, which makes them more likely to shove over a raise, but my recent raises have been successful in taking down the blinds without a contest. I will maintain my aggressive image and raise to 300, which is consistent with my previous betting sizing.

I am in the small blind with 6s-Th, which is a weak offsuit hand. Calling a raise out of position with this holding is generally negative expected value and difficult to play post-flop. Folding is the most prudent decision.

We are in the Big Blind with pocket threes and face a 3bb open from the Button. While our stack is relatively short at around 20 big blinds, we are getting excellent pot odds to defend. Although we don't have the deepest implied odds for purely set-mining, against a wide Button opening range, our hand holds decent equity. We will call to see a flop and evaluate, looking to continue on favorable low boards or if we hit a set.

The flop of Q-K-8 heavily favors the pre-flop raiser's range, which contains plenty of high broadway cards. Our pocket threes completely missed the board and are very likely behind. Checking to the pre-flop aggressor is the standard and safest play. We are prepared to fold to any continuation bet.

I have flopped top pair with King-high. My opponent, Pro, is playing on a very short stack (approximately 15 big blinds remaining). On a board of Qc-Kd-8s, I am ahead of most of Pro's calling range from the big blind, which includes hands like Qx, 8x, and various straight draws like JT. Given the low stack-to-pot ratio, I want to start building the pot to eventually get Pro's remaining chips in. A bet of 300 (roughly half the pot) is a standard continuation bet that keeps the pressure on and extracts value from weaker hands or draws.

The flop is Q-K-8, which heavily connects with the pre-flop raiser's broadway-heavy range. Our pocket threes are an extreme underpair, have virtually no equity to improve, and are almost certainly behind. Facing a continuation bet, the only sensible move is to fold and preserve our remaining short stack for a better opportunity.

My hand, 2s 9c, is extremely weak and has poor playability. In a three-handed game, even on the button, there is no strategic incentive to play such a marginal holding. Folding is the disciplined and correct action to preserve my stack.

I have 74o, a very weak hand. I am in the small blind and fairly short-stacked at around 17 BBs. There is no reason to invest any more chips or try to steal with such poor equity against the big blind's wide defending range. Folding is the standard and correct decision.
I have 82 offsuit, which is one of the worst hands in Texas Hold'em. Since I am first to act on the Button, folding is the clear optimal move to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

I am in the Small Blind with 9s 6c. This is a weak offsuit hand. While I only need to call 50 more to see the flop against the Big Blind, I will be out of position for the rest of the hand. Given my significant chip lead and the fact that 9-6 offsuit doesn't play well post-flop, I'll choose to fold and wait for a better spot to apply pressure.

I am on the button with 5h 2s, which is essentially the bottom of the starting hand range. Pro in the Big Blind is very short-stacked with only about 16.7 big blinds, making them likely to shove over a button open. There is no strategic value in playing this hand given the current stack sizes and my hand strength.

With the button (Flash) folding, I am in the small blind against the big blind (Pro). Holding an Ace-high suited hand (Ac 6c) provides a significant equity advantage in a heads-up blind-versus-blind scenario. Raising is the optimal play to exert pressure, attempt to take down the blinds immediately, or secure the initiative to play post-flop.
I am in the Big Blind with a very weak hand (93s). The Small Blind raised to 3 BBs. With a short stack of around 17 BBs, 93s is far too weak to defend or shove, so folding is the only viable option.

I am on the button with pocket deuces, which is a strong enough hand to open the pot in this position. A standard raise to 300 allows me to take the initiative and potentially win the blinds or see a favorable flop in position.
54o is a very weak hand. Facing a 3x raise from the button and being out of position with a short stack (16 BBs), folding is the only viable option.

I am in the Big Blind with Q3o, which is a very weak hand. Flashlight opened to 300 from the button, and the Small Blind folded. Although I am getting decent pot odds (200 to call into a pot of 450), Q3o plays poorly out of position and has very little post-flop equity. 8s Td is a weak offsuit one-gapper. With 16 big blinds, I need to be careful with my chip stack and wait for a better spot to try and steal or shove. Open folding is the correct and safest play here.

With the Button (Pro) folding, the action is on me in the Small Blind. J9o is a solid starting hand for a blind-vs-blind battle. I will raise to 300 to put pressure on the Big Blind (Flashlight) and take the initiative in the hand. Limping allows the Big Blind too much equity for free, and J9o is strong enough to play for a raise.

I am in the big blind with 5s-9d, facing a raise to 300 from the small blind. While 5-9 offsuit is a weak holding, the pot odds (200 to win a total pot of 400) offer a 2:1 price, making it statistically acceptable to defend my big blind and see a flop heads-up. I will call the 200 chips to complete the bet.

As the pre-flop aggressor, this King-high board is very favorable for my perceived range. Even though I have missed the flop with Jack-high, a continuation bet is highly likely to force a fold from many of the Big Blind's hands that also missed. I will bet 300, which is half the pot, to represent a King or a strong pocket pair and take down the pot now.

I have 9-high on a K-6-4 rainbow board with no legitimate draws or showdown value. The opponent's continuation bet indicates strength, and continuing here with absolute air would be a mistake. Folding is the only sensible option.

I am on the button with J6o. The player in the Big Blind (Pro) is very short-stacked with only 15.25 BB (1,525 chips). If I raise, I am inviting a shove from the short stack. Given the pot odds, I might be forced to call with a marginal hand that is likely an underdog. Since I have a significant chip lead and want to minimize unnecessary variance, I will fold this weak hand and wait for a better spot to put pressure on my opponents or bust the short stack.

Holding Jack-Six offsuit in the small blind against the big blind is a weak position. Given the poor hand strength and the fact that I will be out of position for the rest of the hand, folding is the most disciplined and prudent choice to preserve my stack.

K2 offsuit is a very weak starting hand, offering poor equity even in position on the button. With a stack of over 12,000, there is no need to play marginal hands, especially when a short-stacked opponent is in the small blind. Folding is the most disciplined and optimal play here.
I have Q3o in the Small Blind. With a stack of 16.25 BBs, I need to preserve my chips for a better opportunity to shove or play a stronger hand. Q3o is too weak to play, even blind versus blind.

With ATh on the Button and a stack of just over 16 big blinds (1625), this is a perfect spot for an open-shove. Moving all-in maximizes our fold equity against the blinds, forcing them to fold out hands with decent equity, while guaranteeing we see all five cards if called. It also avoids awkward post-flop situations where we might miss the board and face pressure with a low SPR (stack-to-pot ratio).

Pro (Button) has shoved for 16.25 BBs (1625 total). I am in the Small Blind with K6o. I need to call 1575 into a total potential pot of 3350, requiring approximately 47% equity. Against a standard button shoving range for this depth (which includes all pairs, most Aces, and several King-high or Queen-high hands), K6o generally has around 42-44% equity, making it a slightly negative expected value call. Furthermore, calling allows the Big Blind (Flashlight) to potentially shove over the top or overcall, which significantly decreases my hand's playability and expected value. Folding is the most prudent play to preserve my chip lead and avoid a high-variance marginal situation.

I am in the Big Blind with Q7 offsuit, which is a very weak hand to defend against a large raise of 1625 from the Button (Pro). Calling 1525 into a pot of 1775 with this holding is mathematically poor as the hand plays poorly post-flop and is dominated by the opponent's raising range. Folding is the disciplined and correct strategic choice.

I am on the button with 9d 3h, which is a very weak hand. Pro in the Big Blind is short-stacked with only about 16.75 big blinds, making them likely to defend aggressively or shove over a raise. There is no strategic value in playing such a weak hand in this position against these stack depths.

I am in the small blind with 2h7c, which is a very weak hand. Given the poor quality of these cards, investing more chips into the pot is not mathematically sound. Folding and conceding the small blind is the most disciplined action, preserving my stack for a better opportunity.
My hand, 3h 9d, is extremely weak, unsuited, and disconnected. There is no strategic value in playing this holding even in position, so I will fold to avoid wasting chips on a marginal situation.

With QJo in the Small Blind and a short stack of around 17.75 BB, I have a strong enough hand to open with a raise and try to steal the blinds. A standard raise puts pressure on the Big Blind while leaving me enough chips to fold if I face a 3-bet shove, thereby avoiding risking my entire tournament life on a marginal offsuit broadway hand.

I am in the Big Blind with 9c 5h, which is a very weak hand. Pro (Small Blind) has a very short stack of 1,575 and has raised to 250. While the price to call is relatively cheap (150 more into a 350 pot), 95o has very poor equity against almost any range Pro would be raising with from the SB, especially given their desperation as a short stack. There is no need to gift chips to a short stack with trash hands when I have a significant chip lead. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

I am on the button with Queen-Deuce offsuit, which is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. With a stack of about 19 big blinds, I cannot afford to open-raise or limp with such garbage. I need to preserve my remaining chips for a much better spot to either shove or play a stronger range.

With the button folding, I am in the Small Blind with 9d 3d. While suited, this is a very weak hand (9-3). The Big Blind, Flashlight, has a significant stack and has shown a willingness to defend or play pots. Given that I am out of position for the rest of the hand and the hand strength is low, folding is the most prudent play to preserve my chip lead and avoid unnecessary confrontations with the other large stack.

I am on the button with Q2 offsuit. While I have a large stack, the player in the Big Blind (Pro) is extremely short-stacked with only 18.25 BB. This makes them very likely to shove all-in over any raise I make. Q2o is a very weak hand and does not perform well against a desperate short-stack's shoving range. Folding is the safest and most standard play here to avoid giving the short stack a chance to double up through me with a marginal hand.

I am in the small blind and the button has folded, leaving me heads-up against the big blind (Pro), who has a very short stack of 1825. Raising to 300 is a standard move to put pressure on the short-stacked big blind and potentially take down the pot pre-flop or gain positional advantage.

With a short stack of around 18 big blinds and a very weak hand like Ten-Five suited, defending against a Small Blind raise is not profitable. Calling would consume a significant portion of my stack with a hand that plays poorly post-flop, and it's too weak to 3-bet shove as a bluff. Folding is the optimal play to preserve my chips for a better spot.

I am in the button position with 8h 3c, which is a very weak starting hand with low equity. Given that I want to preserve my stack and there is no incentive to play such a marginal holding, the most strategic and disciplined decision is to fold.

With T6o in the Small Blind and a short stack of about 17 big blinds, this hand is extremely weak. Furthermore, it is not a viable candidate for a pre-flop shove steal given the lack of high-card strength or suitedness. Folding is the clear optimal choice.

With around 18 big blinds left, my stack is in the danger zone, but 98o is too weak of an offsuit connector to open or shove from the button. Against two deep-stacked opponents in the blinds who can easily call a shove or defend against a min-raise, the best play is to fold and wait for a stronger hand to commit my chips.

I am in the Small Blind with Queen-Eight offsuit. Since the button (Pro) has folded, I am only up against the Big Blind (Flashlight). Q8o is a relatively strong hand in a blind-versus-blind scenario. I will raise to 300 to put pressure on the Big Blind and potentially take down the pot pre-flop. With a significant chip lead, I can afford to play aggressively against the other big stack while the short stack is out of the hand.

I am in the Big Blind with a very weak hand (4h 6d). Facing a raise from the Small Blind, calling would require me to commit additional chips with a hand that has very low equity and will likely be difficult to play out of position post-flop. Folding is the disciplined and standard play.

I am on the button with Q4o. While 3-handed play requires a wider range, Q4o is significantly below average. 4-3 offsuit is an extremely weak hand with minimal connectivity or potential. Given the poor position and the stack sizes, folding is the most disciplined and mathematically sound decision, especially since I am already at the end of the tournament session.

I am on the button with 8d 5d, which are suited connectors. Given the stack depths and the fact that I am in late position, I will attempt to steal the blinds with a standard open-raise of 300 to take control of the hand.
Facing a raise from the button, J6s is too weak to play from the small blind, especially being out of position and with a short stack of around 18 BBs. Folding is the only viable option.

I am in the Big Blind with 83 suited. Flashlight has raised to 300 from the Button, and the Small Blind (Pro) has folded. With Pro being extremely short-stacked at 1775 chips, there is no need to take unnecessary risks or enter marginal spots to defend the blind with bottom-tier cards. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity to utilize my chip lead.

I have Pocket Kings on the Button, which is an absolute premium hand. With 17.75 BBs remaining, I need to extract maximum value. A standard open to 250 will look like a normal raise, potentially inducing a 3-bet from the aggressive big stacks in the blinds. I am very happy to get all my chips in pre-flop if either of them plays back at me.

I am holding QdTd, a strong suited broadway hand that plays very well in a three-handed game. Pro, on the button, has raised to 250 and is currently short-stacked with only 1,525 chips (about 15 big blinds). My hand has significant equity against a wide button-opening range, and the raise helps me take control of the hand and potentially win the pot immediately.

I am in the Big Blind with 7-10 offsuit, a very weak hand. Facing an open from the Button and a strong 3-bet from the Small Blind, calling 700 chips out of position would be highly unprofitable. Folding is the standard and correct play here.

I hold Pocket Kings, the second best starting hand in Texas Hold'em. Facing a 3-bet to 800 from the Small Blind and with only 1525 chips left in my stack, the pot is already huge and I am entirely committed. Shoving all-in is the absolute best play to maximize value against their 3-betting range, which includes many worse hands like AK, QQ, JJ, or TT that might call my shove.

Pro has moved all-in for a total of 1,775 chips. I have already committed 800 chips to the pot, so it costs me an additional 975 chips to call. The total pot after my call would be 3,650 chips. I need approximately 26.7% equity to make this call profitable. Given that Pro is short-stacked and playing three-handed, his shoving range is likely very wide, including many hands that QdTd performs well against (lower suited connectors, some Ax, and small pairs). Even against a relatively tight range, QdTd usually retains around 30-40% equity. Given the excellent pot odds and the strength of my suited broadway hand, I have a clear call.

I have a strong hand (AQo) on the button in a three-handed game. I will raise to 300 to build the pot and put pressure on the blinds. Given Pro's relatively short stack, I am prepared to call a shove if it comes to that, as AQo is well ahead of an average shoving range in this position.

76o is a weak hand to play from the small blind against a button raise. Playing out of position with this hand is generally negative expected value. I will fold to conserve my stack for better opportunities.

K5o is a weak hand to play from the Big Blind, even against a Button raise. The low kicker gives it poor playability and reverse implied odds post-flop. The best move here is to fold and preserve the stack for a better spot.
I am on the button with K9 offsuit, which is a playable hand for a steal attempt in a 3-handed game. I will open with a standard raise to 300 to pressure the blinds.

ATo from the small blind against a 3x button open is a marginal spot. Playing it out of position if we call is difficult, and flatting also invites the big blind to squeeze. Folding is the safest play here to preserve our stack for better opportunities.

I am in the Big Blind with 4h 2c, which is one of the weakest starting hands in poker. Flashlight raised from the button to 300. Given the stage of the tournament and my healthy stack, there is no need to defend with such a marginal hand.

With K5o on the Button and a relatively short stack compared to the opponents, this hand is too weak and easily dominated to open-raise. A simple fold is the most standard and mathematically sound decision.

With pocket sevens in the small blind and the action folded to me, I have a strong starting hand for a heads-up situation against the big blind. I will raise to 300 to build the pot and take the initiative, which is a standard opening size in this tournament.

K-6 offsuit is a weak holding, even in the Big Blind against a Small Blind open. Despite the decent pot odds, playing such a marginal hand out of position (OOP) against an aggressive opponent is strategically unsound. Conserving chips for a better opportunity is the disciplined play.

I am on the button with 9s7s, a solid speculative hand in a 3-handed game. With the chip lead, I want to maintain pressure on the blinds. Raising to 300 is a standard open that allows me to take control of the pot or win it pre-flop, while also providing good post-flop playability if either opponent calls.

I am in the small blind with King-six offsuit. This hand is too weak to defend against a raise from the button, as it performs poorly post-flop and I will be out of position for the rest of the hand. Folding is the most disciplined and standard play here to conserve my stack.
Holding 5-2 offsuit, I have the worst starting hand in poker. Facing a raise to 300 from the Button, folding is the only viable option to preserve my stack for a better opportunity.

With Q4 offsuit on the button, I have a reasonable chance to steal the blinds. Raising is a standard move to take control of the hand or play in position post-flop, especially given my relatively strong stack position compared to the other players.

Q8o is a weak offsuit holding that plays poorly out of position. I am in the Small Blind facing a button open from Flashlight, and there is still the Big Blind left to act. With about 33.5 BBs, calling or 3-betting with this hand is simply not profitable, so folding is the best play.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten-Six offsuit. Flashlight raised to 250 from the Button, and the Small Blind folded. I need to call 150 more to see a flop in a pot that will be 550. This gives me pot odds of nearly 3.7-to-1, meaning I need roughly 27% equity to continue. Ten-Six offsuit has sufficient equity against a typical wide button opening range, making this a standard defend despite being out of position post-flop.

I have flopped middle pair (Tens) on an Ace-high board. As the Big Blind in a heads-up pot against the pre-flop aggressor (Flashlight), the standard play is to check to the raiser. My hand has some showdown value but is vulnerable to any Ace or better. I will check and evaluate the situation based on Flashlight's action and bet sizing.

The board is A-T-7, which hits the big blind's calling range fairly well, including hands with an Ace or a Ten. With only Queen-high, I have very little equity to call a raise if I bet and get check-raised. Checking back allows me to control the pot size and keep my hand's value realized if I hit a Queen or running cards, while avoiding putting more chips into a pot where I am likely behind.

On the turn, I have improved to two pair (Tens and Sixes). The pre-flop aggressor (Flashlight) checked back on an Ace-high flop, which often indicates they do not have a strong Ace or are choosing to play a medium-strength hand/bluff-catcher passively. By leading out now, I can extract value from weaker Tens, Sevens, or any Ace that was playing cautiously. It also protects my hand against potential straight draws (like 89) or the newly arrived heart flush draw. A bet of roughly 65% of the pot is appropriate to build the pot while still being likely to get called by second-best hands.

I have Queen-high with no draw on an Ace-high board. The opponent is betting into me on the turn after checking the flop. My hand has no showdown value and no equity to improve significantly against an Ace or any pair, so calling would be a losing play. Folding is the standard disciplined decision here.

With pocket fives on the Button and a 33.5 BB stack, I have a standard opening hand. Raising to 2.5x (250) applies pressure to the blinds, allows me to win the pot uncontested, and gives me position post-flop if called.

I am in the Small Blind with K7o. The Button (Pro) has raised to 250. Pro is the short stack with only about 3,100 remaining. K7o is a marginal hand against a Button opening range, but given the pot odds (calling 200 to win a pot that will be 600) and the fact that I am out of position against a short stack, I could either call or fold. However, looking at the previous hand history, Pro has been playing relatively tight-aggressive. Calling out of position against a short stack who might be looking for a spot to shove post-flop isn't ideal. But at these blinds, K7o is often strong enough to defend. I will call the 200 and see a flop, keeping the pot manageable against the short stack.

I am in the Big Blind with King-Jack offsuit. The button (Pro) raised to 250, and the Small Blind (Flash) called. The pot is 600, and I only need to call 150 more. Given the pot odds and my holding, this is an easy call to see a flop in position for the rest of the hand.

I have completely missed this flop with King-high (K7o). The board contains an Ace and a Ten, which are very likely to have hit the ranges of either the pre-flop raiser (Pro) or the Big Blind (Flashlight). Being out of position and having no equity or draws, there is no reason to lead out. I will check and most likely fold to any significant aggression from my opponents.

The flop of Ace-high is disadvantageous for my King-Jack holding, though I do have a gutshot straight draw. Since there are two opponents in the hand and an Ace is very likely to have connected with at least one of their ranges, checking is the most strategic play to control the size of the pot and see the turn for free or re-evaluate depending on their actions.

With two opponents calling pre-flop and an Ace and Ten on the flop, my pocket fives are likely behind, as this board connects well with their calling ranges. A continuation bet into two players on an Ace-high board is risky and unlikely to succeed often enough to be profitable, especially with my relatively short stack. Checking allows me to control the pot, avoid unnecessary risk, and potentially see a free turn to hit a set.

On this very dry board (As 8h Tc 3d), I have no pair and no draw with King-high. Everyone checked the flop, which suggests no one has a very strong hand, but with two opponents still in the hand, including the short-stacked pre-flop raiser (Pro), a bluff is unlikely to succeed without equity. Checking is the most prudent play to see if I can get to a free showdown or fold if someone else shows interest in the pot.

I am in the Big Blind with King-Jack offsuit. The board is Ace-Eight-Ten-Three, and the action has checked through to the turn. Given that an Ace is on the board and there are two opponents, my King-high is currently weak, but I still have a gutshot straight draw to the Broadway straight. Since both opponents have checked, checking behind is the best strategic play to see a free river card and keep the pot size manageable, as a bluff here is unlikely to succeed against two opponents on such a coordinated board.

With two checks ahead of me on the turn, it's possible my pocket fives are still good, but betting here mostly folds out worse hands while getting called by better (like an 8, a 10, or a slow-played Ace). The board is rainbow, so there are no flush draws to charge. Checking behind allows me to control the size of the pot and take a free river card, maximizing my chances of getting to a cheap showdown or hitting a two-outer.

On the river, I have improved to a pair of sevens (bottom pair). The action has been checked through on both the flop and the turn, which suggests that no one has a strong hand like an Ace or a high Ten. However, in a three-way pot, even a pair of sevens is relatively weak. By checking, I allow for the possibility of seeing a cheap showdown. If I bet, I am unlikely to be called by worse hands and might be raised by an Eight or a Ten that was playing cautiously. I will check and see how my opponents react.

I am in the Big Blind with King-Jack offsuit on a board of Ace-Eight-Ten-Three-Seven. The pot has been checked through on the flop and turn, and the small blind has checked on the river. My hand is King-high, which loses to any pair. Since I am last to act before the button, checking allows me to get to showdown for free, avoiding a potentially unnecessary bluff that would likely be called by any pair that connected with the board.

Both opponents have checked to me on the river. My pocket fives have some showdown value, potentially beating missed broadway hands or straight draws like QJ or J9. If I bet, I'm very unlikely to get called by worse hands, and I might get called by a sneaky 7, 8, or even a slow-played Ace or Ten. Because there's no value to be gained from worse hands and no real need to turn this pair into a bluff, checking behind to take the hand to showdown is the optimal play to conserve my stack.

I am on the button with 9h 4h. While it is a suited hand, 9-4 is quite weak. Pro, in the Big Blind, is short-stacked with only 3000 chips (30 BBs), making him more likely to shove over a button raise. Given my significant chip lead and the poor quality of my hand, there is no need to risk chips or induce a confrontation with the short stack. I will fold and wait for a better spot to apply pressure.

I am in the small blind with A3o after the button (Flash) folded. The big blind (Pro) is short-stacked with only 3000 chips. While A3o is a mediocre hand, raising from the small blind is a standard strategy to steal the blinds and put pressure on the short stack, especially since my stack (11725) is significantly larger. I will raise to 300 to steal the pot or force the short stack to make a difficult decision.

I am in the Big Blind with a weak suited two-gapper, 9d5d. The Small Blind raised to 3x (300). Calling with 30 BBs and a marginal hand is a bleeding proposition. Folding is the most profitable and standard play here to preserve my short stack for better spots.

K3 offsuit is a weak hand in a 3-handed game, even from the button. There is no incentive to play this hand as I have a healthy stack and it is likely to be dominated by the ranges of the opponents. Folding is the disciplined play.

With A7o in the small blind and the button folding, I have a strong hand to attack the big blind. My stack is around 30 big blinds, which is too deep for an open-shove but perfectly suited for a standard raise. I'll make it 3x to pressure the big blind, aiming to steal the pot directly while maintaining a decent Ace to play post-flop if called.

In this blind-vs-blind situation, Pro (SB) has raised to 3 BB with a relatively short stack of 27 BB. My hand, Queen-Ten offsuit (QTo), is well above the average defending range in the Big Blind against a Small Blind open. I am getting 2:1 odds on a call (200 to win a total pot of 400). Given the stack depth and the strength of my hand, calling is the most standard and effective play to see a flop and utilize my position for the rest of the hand without unnecessarily inflating the pot pre-flop against a short-stacked opponent.

I flopped top pair with an Ace kicker, but the board is monotone in clubs and I don't hold a single club. This makes my hand extremely vulnerable. Leading out with a bet here could easily bloat the pot against a made flush or a strong flush draw that has massive equity against me. Checking allows me to control the size of the pot, keep Flash's weaker hands in his range, and evaluate his action. If he checks back, we see a turn for free; if he bets, I can decide to call or fold based on his sizing.

On this monochromatic board of 4c 2c 7c, I hold the Qc, which gives me the second-nut flush draw. Pro, the small blind, has a relatively short stack (27 BBs remaining) and has checked to me. While betting could potentially end the hand now, checking back allows me to realize my equity for free and potentially hit a very strong hand on the turn without risking a check-raise from a short-stacked opponent. Given that I have the Qc, I am happy to see a free card and keep the pot manageable.

The turn pairs the board with a 4, which is unlikely to have improved Flash's range given the pre-flop call and flop check. My pair of sevens with an Ace kicker is very likely the best hand here since the flop checked through, making a flopped flush highly improbable for Flash. I need to bet to protect my hand from single club draws, deny free equity, and extract value from worse hands like underpairs or weak floats.

On the turn 4s, the board pairs and Pro leads for 300 into a 600 pot. I hold the Qc, which gives me the second-nut flush draw on this 4c 2c 7c board. Pro is short-stacked with only about 24 BBs remaining. Calling is the most logical choice here as I am getting 3:1 pot odds to realize my equity. Raising doesn't accomplish much against a short stack who might be leading with a marginal made hand or a draw themselves, and calling allows me to potentially hit my flush on the river or even win with Queen-high if the action checks through on certain runouts. I have a very strong draw and sufficient odds to continue.

The river pairs the 7, giving me Sevens full of Fours, which is effectively the nuts (only losing to the extremely unlikely quad 4s or 2s). I need to extract maximum value from Flash. Since the pot is 1200 and my remaining stack is 2400, going all-in for 2x the pot might be too polarizing and could fold out his marginal made hands or flushes. A bet of 800 is a standard two-thirds pot size that targets crying calls from flushes, an overpair, or a 4x that just improved to a smaller full house.

On this double-paired and three-club board (4c 2c 7c 4s 7h), my Queen-high (Qc Td) has failed to improve into a flush. Pro followed up a turn bet with a significant river bet of 800 into a 1200 pot. Given that the board is double-paired, any pocket pair, any 2, 4, or 7, or even an Ace-high hand beats me. Pro's line of betting into the lead on both the turn and river suggests a hand with showdown value or a better high card. Calling with Queen-high is extremely unlikely to result in a win, and raising to bluff is risky against an opponent who has committed a significant portion of their stack. The most disciplined play is to fold and preserve my chips.

8-2 offsuit is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. As the first to act on the Button with a 36bb stack, there is absolutely no reason to play this hand, especially against two opponents who cover me significantly.

I am in the Small Blind with KTo after the Button folded. In a blind-vs-blind situation, King-Ten is a very strong starting hand. Raising is the standard play here to put pressure on the Big Blind and take control of the pot. I will raise to 300 chips (3x the big blind).

I am in the Big Blind with Q7o. Flash, in the Small Blind, raised to 300. With 200 more to call into a pot of 400, the pot odds are attractive enough to defend, especially against a wide stealing range from the Small Blind. I will call and see a flop.

I hold King-high and a gutshot straight draw on a board with an Ace and a Queen. Since I was the pre-flop aggressor, this flop is very favorable for my perceived range. I will lead out with a continuation bet of 300 to represent top pair and capitalize on my range advantage while maintaining equity with my draw.

I have middle pair (Queens) with a weak kicker on an Ace-high board. While the Ace is a scary card, Flash is the Small Blind and is betting a small amount (300 into 900), which looks like a standard continuation bet. With 4-to-1 pot odds, I have sufficient equity to call and re-evaluate on the turn.

I am currently holding King-high with a gutshot straight draw on a board of Ace-high. I led the flop with a continuation bet of 300, and the Big Blind called. The turn is a 5 of spades, which doesn't significantly change the board texture. Since the opponent called on the flop, it is highly likely they have an Ace, a Queen, or a draw. Checking here allows me to control the pot size and potentially see a free river card to hit my straight draw, rather than risking a large amount on a second bluff against a range that likely contains many calling hands like top pair.

Flash checked the turn, indicating potential weakness or a trap on this Ace-high board. Given my marginal pair of Queens with a weak kicker, betting may lead to getting check-raised by a stronger holding. Checking behind is the most prudent play to control the pot size, realize my equity, and take a free river card.

I have improved to second pair with Kings on the river. However, the board is quite coordinated with an Ace and a Queen already present, and a potential straight (JT) is possible. Since I checked the turn, my hand looks like it has showdown value. Checking the river allows me to realize that value against missed draws or mid-range hands without risking a raise from an Ace or a straight. If the opponent bets, I will have to evaluate the size, but checking is the best way to control the pot here and reach a showdown with a marginal hand.

I am in the Big Blind with a pair of Queens on a board of 8s-Qc-Ah-5s-Kh. Flash, the aggressor, checked both the turn and the river. Given the presence of an Ace and a King on the board, my pair of Queens is likely a bluff-catcher or a showdown-value hand, but it is unlikely to be called by worse hands if I bet. Checking behind allows me to go to showdown safely and avoid getting check-raised or value-bet into if Flash happens to be trapping with an Ace or King. Betting does not provide significant value here.

I am on the button with 96 suited in a three-handed game. This is a standard opening range hand to apply pressure to the blinds. I currently hold the chip lead, and Pro is relatively short-stacked at 35BB, making them less likely to defend loosely. I will raise to 250 to pick up the blinds or play the hand in position.

Suited connectors like 5s4s are good candidates for a call in the small blind against a button raise. The pot odds (calling 200 to win a total of 400) make this a mathematically sound decision to see a flop. I want to keep the pot manageable and use my positional advantage (though limited as I am first to act post-flop) to realize my equity if I hit a favorable board.

I am in the Big Blind with pocket fours. The Button raised to 2.5x and the Small Blind called. I'm getting excellent pot odds (150 to win 600) to set mine here. If I flop a set, I have the potential to win a significant pot from two opponents. If I miss, I can easily fold to any post-flop action. With a stack of 3500, my implied odds justify the 150 chip call.

The flop of 3h-Kc-Js is very disconnected from my 5s-4s. I have no pair, no draw, and no real equity in this hand. Betting into two opponents on this dry, high-card-heavy board would be a transparent bluff. Checking is the most logical and conservative play, allowing me to evaluate the action from my opponents or fold if someone bets strongly.

I missed my set on a flop that features two high cards (King and Jack), which heavily favor the pre-flop raising and calling ranges of my opponents. My pocket fours have very little showdown value and are highly vulnerable in a multi-way pot. Since I am out of position, checking is the safest and most standard play, with the intention of folding to any significant bet.

On a board of Kc-Js-3h, I have no direct connection to the community cards, holding only 9d-6d. Both the Small Blind (Flashlight) and Big Blind (Pro) have checked to me. This board texture heavily favors the calling ranges of the blinds, who likely hold many Kings, Jacks, and straight draws (like QT or T9). Given that I am facing two opponents, a continuation bet is less likely to succeed, especially with Pro being relatively short-stacked and potentially looking for a spot to commit. I will check behind to take a free turn card and see how the board develops.

The board (3h, Kc, Js, 9s) is reasonably coordinated, and I have a flush draw with my 5s-4s. Since the flop was checked around by all three players, it is unlikely that anyone has a monster hand, but betting into two opponents with only a flush draw is risky and potentially ineffective. Checking is the most prudent play here, as it allows me to realize my equity for free if no one bets, or to re-evaluate if one of my opponents decides to make a move.

With pocket fours on a board of 3h Kc Js 9s, my hand has virtually no showdown value and is vulnerable to many draws and overcards. The action checked through on the flop, but betting into two opponents on this scary turn would be a pure bluff with no equity if called. Checking is the safest and only logical play, planning to fold to any bet.

I have turned middle pair (9s) on a board of Kc-Js-3h-9s. Both opponents checked the flop and have now checked the turn. While my hand has improved and now has showdown value, it is still vulnerable to Kings and Jacks, which my opponents could easily have and be playing cautiously. By checking behind, I control the pot size and ensure I see the river for free without risking a check-raise from the short-stacked Big Blind. This is the safest way to realize my equity in position.

My hand (5s 4s) has no showdown value on a board of 3h-Kc-Js-9s-Tc. With a straight possible on the board (Q-K-J-T-9) and multiple opponents who have checked throughout the hand, there is no chance that a bet would force a fold from a better hand. Checking is the only logical move to avoid losing more chips when I have effectively zero equity.

The river brings a Ten, completing a very scary board of 3-K-J-9-T with a four-card straight. My pocket fours have absolutely no showdown value here. Betting into two opponents would be a complete bluff, and given the board texture, it's very likely someone will call with at least a pair or a straight if they have a Queen. The safest and most logical play is to check and prepare to fold if anyone bets.

On a board of Kc-Js-3h-9s-Tc, I have a pair of nines. The board is quite connected, and any Queen now makes a straight. Both Flashlight and Pro have checked through every street. While my pair of nines has some showdown value, it is unlikely to get called by a worse hand if I bet. Conversely, many hands that have me beaten (Kings, Jacks, Tens, or any Queen) might have played passively given the way the hand developed. Checking behind allows me to realize my showdown value in a small pot without risking a check-raise from the short-stacked Pro or a call from a better hand from Flashlight.

Holding Jack-Six offsuit is a very weak hand in a 3-handed game. Despite being on the button, this hand lacks the potential to form strong made hands or draws consistently enough to justify putting chips into the pot. Preserving my stack for a more favorable hand is the priority.

Q4o is a very weak hand from the small blind. I'm the shortest stack with 33 BBs, so there's no reason to bleed chips by limping or raising with trash here. A fold is the safest and most standard play.

K9o is a marginal offsuit hand. Although we have the advantage of being on the button, our relatively short stack of 33 BBs means we should be careful about opening too wide and getting into tricky post-flop situations against deep-stacked opponents who can easily put us to the test. Folding here preserves our stack for better stealing opportunities or stronger value hands in the remaining few hands.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Ten offsuit (ATo). Since the Button (Pro) has folded, the action is on me. ATo is a very strong hand in a blind-vs-blind situation. I will put in a standard open raise of 3x the big blind (300 total) to exert pressure and build the pot with my range advantage.

Holding Q3o in the Big Blind against a raise from the Small Blind is a weak position. This hand has poor equity and playability, so it is best to fold and conserve chips rather than chasing a marginal spot.

On the button in a three-handed game, 9-4 suited is a very weak hand. While I have a significant chip lead, the big blind (Pro) has a relatively short stack of 3,200 chips (32 BBs), making him more likely to shove over a button open. Raising with such a weak hand risks losing chips to a short-stack shove or getting into a difficult post-flop situation against the small blind. Given the hand strength and the tournament stage, folding is the most prudent and disciplined play.

With the button folding and me in the small blind, I am heads-up against the big blind. Although Queen-Six offsuit is a marginal hand, it is standard to complete the small blind in this heads-up scenario to see a flop cheaply, especially with the big blind being a short stack. I will call to complete the big blind.

Flashlight limps from the small blind. I hold a weak Ace (A3o) in the big blind. While I could raise to attack the limp, A3o plays marginally in inflated pots, and checking allows us to see a free flop in position. This keeps the pot manageable for our 32 BB stack while fully realizing our hand's equity.

With Queen-high on an Ace-high board featuring two diamonds, my hand has minimal equity. Attempting a continuation bet here would be a pure bluff that is unlikely to succeed against the Big Blind's range, which likely includes many Aces. Checking is the most prudent action to control the pot size, see a turn card for free if possible, and avoid committing more chips with a weak holding.

I have top pair with a weak kicker on a fairly wet board featuring flush and straight draws. Flashlight checking to me presents a good opportunity to bet. A half-pot bet will extract value from draws, protect my equity against overcards or backdoor draws, and define my opponent's range while keeping the pot manageable given my weak kicker.

The flop is extremely disadvantageous for my hand, as I have nothing but Queen-high on an Ace-high board. My opponent (the Big Blind) checked the option pre-flop and has now bet on an Ace-high flop, which is a very strong card for his potential range. Continuing with this hand would be throwing away chips on a board where I have zero equity. Folding is the only logical decision to preserve my stack for a more favorable situation.

I am in the button position with a pocket pair of threes (3c, 3h). This is a strong starting hand for late position in a three-handed game. I want to take initiative and build the pot or potentially take it down pre-flop. Raising is superior to calling here to define the action and narrow the field against the blinds.

I am in the Small Blind with 8s3d, which is an extremely weak and disconnected offsuit hand. Facing a raise from the Button, I have no reason to get involved. I will fold to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

I am in the Big Blind with Kh 5h. Flashlight raised from the button to 300, and Pro folded. The pot currently has 450 chips, and it costs me 200 more to call. K5 suited is a standard defending hand in the Big Blind against a button open, especially given the excellent pot odds (2.25:1). Calling allows me to realize my equity and play a pot in a situation where my hand has decent post-flop playability and nut potential (flush).

I am in the Big Blind with King-high on a Queen-high flop. Since Flashlight was the pre-flop aggressor from the button, they likely have a range advantage on this board. My hand has very little equity, with only backdoor hearts and overcard potential. The standard play here is to check and see how the opponent reacts.

The board (2s, 6c, Qd) is relatively dry and disconnected. As the pre-flop aggressor holding a small pocket pair (3,3), my hand is vulnerable to any overcard. Since the Big Blind checked to me, checking back is the most prudent strategy to control the pot size and realize my equity without over-committing to a vulnerable hand against an opponent's wider range.

The turn card is the Six of Spades, pairing the board. My King-high has some showdown value against his air, but I am likely behind any pocket pair or Queen. Since Flashlight checked back on the flop, their range is likely capped, but could still contain marginal made hands like small pocket pairs or Ace-high. I will check again to see if I can get to a cheap showdown or potentially catch a King on the river. Leading out here doesn't accomplish much as a bluff against the hands he is most likely checking back with.

The board is 2s 6c Qd 6s. My hand is 3c 3h. Since I am in position and have already checked the flop, checking the turn is the standard play to control the pot size with a weak made hand that has little chance of improvement against a calling range. Betting here likely turns my hand into a bluff unnecessarily when I can take a free card to the river.

The board is 2s-6c-Qd-6s-Ac. I have King-high (Kh 5h). The pre-flop aggressor (Flashlight) checked both the flop and the turn, which typically indicates a capped or weak range. However, the Ace on the river is a major scare card. While I could attempt to bluff by representing the Ace or a Six, Flashlight's range also contains many Broadway cards and Aces that might have checked for pot control. Given the small pot size (650) and my position, checking is the most prudent play. My King-high has almost no showdown value, but a bluff here is unlikely to succeed against the marginal made hands (like mid-pairs) that Flashlight is likely to have checked down.

The board is 2s 6c Qd 6s Ac. My hand is 3c 3h. Since the opponent checked to me, I have minimal showdown value and my hand is essentially a bluff-catcher that rarely wins. Betting would not induce a fold from better hands, and I have no reason to represent an Ace, Queen, or a six here. Checking is the most prudent action to keep the pot small and reach showdown.

On the button with J2s, a very weak and disconnected hand. Despite having positional advantage, it's not strong enough to justify an open-raise or a steal attempt with our 33.5 BB stack. Folding is the standard and optimal play.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Eight offsuit, which is a premium hand in a blind-versus-blind scenario. Since the button folded, I am only up against the Big Blind (Flashlight). Raising here is the standard aggressive play to take down the pot immediately or build value against a wider range of hands that the Big Blind might defend with. A raise to 300 (3x the big blind) is a standard opening size.

I am in the big blind facing a raise from the small blind. With 9-10 offsuit, I have decent pot odds to call the 200-chip raise, given the 400-chip pot. Folding is too tight, and raising is overplaying a marginal hand. A call is the standard defensive play in this position.

I am the pre-flop aggressor in a blind-versus-blind pot. The flop of Qc-6d-4s is relatively dry and favors my perceived range, which includes many strong pairs and broadway hands. With Ace-high (Ac 8h), I have some showdown value but would prefer to win the pot immediately. A standard continuation bet of 50% of the pot (300 chips) is an effective way to put pressure on the Big Blind's wider defending range and likely fold out hands that missed the board entirely.

The board is Queen-high (Qc-6d-4s) and I hold 9c-Ts. I have no pair, no legitimate draws, and minimal equity against the opponent's betting range. Calling a bet here is essentially burning chips with air. Folding is the correct strategic decision to preserve my stack.

On the button with Qh Tc in a 3-handed game, this is a standard opening hand. I am the chip leader, and Pro is relatively short with 32.5 BB. Raising to 300 (3x) is a standard play to put pressure on the blinds and take control of the pot in position.

With Ace-Seven suited in the small blind against a button open, I have a strong hand that plays well in position or out of position. 3-betting allows me to take the betting lead, define the button's range, and potentially take down the pot pre-flop. A raise to 900 is an appropriate sizing to isolate and apply pressure.

I am dealt J2o, which is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Facing an open from the Button and a 3-bet from the Small Blind, the action indicates significant strength from both opponents. I have an easy fold.

I opened with Qh Tc from the button, which is a standard play to steal the blinds or play in position. However, Flashlight 3-bet from the small blind to 900. While I have position, Qh Tc is a marginal hand that is often dominated by a 3-betting range (like AQ, AJ, KQ). With only 10 hands left in the tournament and a comfortable chip lead over both opponents, there is no need to take a high-variance risk against the second-largest stack. I will fold and wait for a better spot.
I am in the button position with a very weak starting hand (4h, 6d). There is no incentive to play this hand further given the strength, so I will fold.

With A3 suited in the small blind and the button folded, this is a clear opening spot. Raising to 300 takes the betting lead, puts pressure on the big blind to defend, and gives us a chance to win the pot pre-flop. Even if called, we have a hand with good equity and post-flop playability.

I am in the Big Blind with AJ-offsuit, a premium hand in a three-handed game. The Small Blind (Pro) has raised to 300. Pro is the short stack with about 29.5 big blinds. Against a blind-vs-blind opening range, AJ is significantly ahead. By raising (3-betting) to 900, I exert maximum pressure on the short stack, potentially winning the pot immediately or forcing him to commit a large portion of his stack to see a flop. Given my dominant chip lead, I can afford the aggression to try and eliminate or further cripple the short stack.

Facing a 3-bet from the Big Blind after our Small Blind open, Ad3d is generally too weak to call out of position. Calling would leave us with a very awkward stack-to-pot ratio (SPR around 1.3) post-flop, making it extremely difficult to play unless we flop a strong draw or two pair. While Flash, being the big stack, might be 3-betting wide to apply pressure, 4-bet shoving for our remaining ~30 big blinds is high-variance and risks running into a dominating Ace or a strong pair. Folding is the disciplined play, preserving our 29.5 BB stack for a more profitable situation in the final few hands.

8h Th is a decent suited one-gapper and a reasonable hand to open from the Button to steal the blinds. With around 30 BBs, raising to 2.5x gives us a good price on a steal, while keeping our range sufficiently wide in position.

I am in the Small Blind with a pocket pair of Sixes (6s 6d). The Button (Pro) has raised to 250. Pro is a short stack with only 2700 chips remaining (27 big blinds). Pocket Sixes is a very strong hand in a three-handed game, and I want to put pressure on the short stack or induce a shove that I am willing to call. A 3-bet here is standard to isolate or take the pot down now. Given Pro's stack size, I will raise to 850, which prices them in to commit or fold while remaining well within my own large stack of 15725.

I am in the big blind with a weak, uncoordinated hand (8c, 4c). Facing a button raise and a 3-bet from the small blind, my hand is significantly dominated and the pot odds do not justify calling out of position. Folding is the most disciplined and strategic choice to preserve my stack.

We opened 8h Th from the button as a standard steal attempt, but the Small Blind (Flash), who is the chip leader, 3-bet to 850. We started the hand with 27 BBs, so calling this 8.5 BB 3-bet would commit nearly a third of our stack with a speculative hand. We do not have the implied odds to play a suited one-gapper profitably here, and 4-bet shoving is too reckless against a strong 3-betting range. Folding is the safest and most optimal play.

I am on the button with 7h 4d, which is a very weak hand. The stack sizes are such that Pro is very short with only 2600 chips (26 big blinds), while I am the chip leader. Given the weak nature of my hand and the fact that raising into short stacks often induces shoves, folding is the most prudent and standard play here to preserve my lead as we approach the end of the tournament.

With King-Ten suited in the small blind and the button having folded, I am heads-up against the big blind. This is a strong hand in a blind-versus-blind scenario, and raising to 300 is a standard aggressive move to pressure the big blind and potentially win the pot pre-flop or play a raised pot with a positional advantage.

I am in the Big Blind with a very weak offsuit hand (8h 5d). The Small Blind has raised to 300. With 26 BBs left, I should not be defending such a poor hand against a raise, even from the Small Blind. Folding is the standard and correct play here.

I am holding 3-9 offsuit, which is a very weak hand with poor playability. Even in a late position like the button, there is no strategic justification for investing chips into the pot with such a bottom-tier range. Given the stage of the tournament (hand 145/150) and my stack size, discipline is key to maintaining my position. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

With 5h4h in the small blind and a stack of 25.5 big blinds, limping (calling the extra 50) is a solid, low-risk play. This suited connector plays well post-flop, but raising could unnecessarily bloat the pot out of position against the chip leader, who can easily apply pressure. Limping keeps the pot small and allows us to see a flop cheaply, while giving us an easy fold if the big blind decides to raise.

I have Ah 5d in the big blind. The small blind (Pro) limped in. A5o is a solid hand in this spot, but since Pro has a short stack (25 BB) and the button has folded, checking to see a free flop is the most prudent play. This allows me to see the board without risking a three-bet shove from Pro and keeps the pot manageable while I have a range advantage.

On a dry, disconnected King-high board in a limped pot, checking our gutshot straight draw and backdoor flush draw is the standard play from out of position. It allows us to control the size of the pot against the big blind's wide checking range, and gives us the opportunity to see a free turn card to realize our equity without risking further chips.

On a dry board of Kd-6s-2d, Ace-high still has significant showdown value in a limped pot. Since the Small Blind checked, I will check behind to realize my equity and see the turn for free. There is no reason to bet and risk a check-raise or build a pot with just Ace-high on a board that doesn't offer many draws for me to represent.

The turn brings an 8 of spades, adding a flush draw to the board but not improving our 5-high besides our original 4-outer gutshot to a straight. We have very little equity and no showdown value. Betting out of position into the big blind, who could easily have picked up a pair or a draw, is an unnecessary risk for our 25bb stack. Checking to see a free river or folding to a bet is the most prudent play.

In a limped pot where the Small Blind has checked for the second street in a row, my Ace-high retains significant showdown value. On this dry board (2d Kh 6s 8s), there are few natural bluffs or draws for Pro to have, and a bet from me is unlikely to be called by anything I beat. By checking behind, I control the size of the pot and get a free look at the river, which is a low-risk way to realize my equity against a short-stacked opponent.

On the river, our 5-high has absolutely no showdown value on this 8-8-K-6-2 board, meaning we will lose to almost any two cards our opponent holds if it goes to a showdown. The action has checked through on the flop and turn, strongly suggesting that the big blind lacks a strong hand and is likely holding a missed draw or weak high cards. By making a relatively small bluff of 150 chips, we apply enough pressure to force out better high-card hands like Ace-high, Queen-high, or Jack-high, allowing us to steal the pot with a high success rate.

In a limped pot that was checked through on both the flop and the turn, Pro's river bet of 150 into 200 represents a polarized range or a thin value bet. Given the board (2d Kh 6s 8s 8h), Ace-high has some showdown value, but calling a 75% pot bet requires me to beat more than just air. Pro has generally played a conservative game, and while this could be a 'stab' at a small pot, I lose to any pair (2, 6, 8, K) or even small pocket pairs. Since I am the chip leader and we are near the end of the tournament (hand 145 of 150), there is no need to make a marginal hero call with Ace-high in this spot.

With pocket fours on the button and 27 big blinds remaining, a standard open-raise is the correct play. This gives me a good chance to steal the blinds outright, while allowing me to easily fold to a large 3-bet from the bigger stacks since small pocket pairs lose a lot of value when facing heavy pre-flop aggression. Raising to 2.5x is a cost-effective sizing.

I am in the Small Blind with Queen-Jack offsuit, which is a very strong hand in three-handed play. The Button (Pro) is the short stack with about 24.5 big blinds and has opened for 2.5x. While I have 3-bet Pro successfully in recent hands (#142, #143), I will choose to call this time to see a flop. Calling allows me to keep the pot manageable while still having a hand that plays well against a wide button opening range. If the Big Blind (Flashlight) folds, I will play the pot heads-up against the short stack with a strong starting hand. If I hit the flop, I can use my chip lead to apply pressure.

With Ace-King offsuit in the Big Blind in a 3-handed game, I have a strong premium hand. Given that the button has a relatively short stack of 2450, raising to 950 is an effective 3-bet sizing that isolates the button and builds a substantial pot. This action maximizes my equity and puts significant pressure on the button.

Facing a large 3-bet to 950 from the big blind after the small blind called, my pocket fours are too weak to continue. I don't have the implied odds to set-mine since calling requires committing nearly a third of my remaining stack. Shoving is also too risky; Flashlight is highly likely to call an all-in given his 3-bet sizing, and I'd often be flipping against overcards or crushed by a higher pocket pair. Folding is the most EV-preserving decision, keeping my stack intact to find a better spot in the final few hands.

I originally called the button's open with QJo, a standard play in the Small Blind. However, Flashlight in the Big Blind has now executed a squeeze raise to 950. Pro, the initial raiser, has folded. While the pot odds are tempting (calling 700 into a pot of 1450), QJo is often dominated by Flashlight's 3-betting range (hands like AQ, AJ, KQ, or pocket pairs). Since I am the tournament chip leader and there are only five hands remaining, it is strategically sound to avoid high-variance spots against the second-largest stack, especially when out of position with a hand that doesn't play particularly well against a strong range. I will fold to preserve my lead and wait for better spots.

I am on the button with 8d 5s, which is a very weak hand. We are at Hand #147 of 150, and I currently hold the chip lead. The Big Blind (Pro) is relatively short-stacked (23.5 BB) and may be looking for opportunities to shove and double up. Raising with such a weak hand would be unnecessarily risky at this stage of the tournament, as I would likely have to fold to any aggression. Folding is the most prudent move to preserve my lead and avoid variance with only a few hands remaining.

I am in the Small Blind position with suited connectors (7c, 5c). With the Button having folded, I am heads-up against the Big Blind, who is relatively short-stacked. Completing the small blind to see a flop is a low-risk way to play this hand and potentially outplay the shorter stack if I hit a strong draw or pair on the flop.
